schema.richText:
##Page Types

| Type | Directory | Purpose | |------|-----------|---------| | entity | wiki/entities/ | Named things (people, tools, organizations, datasets) | | concept | wiki/concepts/ | Ideas, techniques, phenomena, frameworks | | source | wiki/sources/ | Papers, articles, talks, books, blog posts | | query | wiki/queries/ | Open questions under active investigation | | comparison | wiki/comparisons/ | Side-by-side analysis of related entities | | synthesis | wiki/synthesis/ | Cross-cutting summaries and conclusions | | overview | wiki/ | High-level project summary (one per project) | | thesis | wiki/thesis/ | Working hypothesis and its evolution over time | | methodology | wiki/methodology/ | Research methods, protocols, and study designs | | finding | wiki/findings/ | Individual empirical results or observations | | report | wiki/reports/ | Generated reports and analysis outputs |

##Naming Conventions

Files: kebab-case.md Entities: match official name where possible (e.g., openai.md, gpt-4.md) Concepts: descriptive noun phrases (e.g., chain-of-thought.md) Sources: author-year-slug.md (e.g., wei-2022-cot.md) Queries: question as slug (e.g., does-scale-improve-reasoning.md) Theses: hypothesis as slug (e.g., scaling-improves-reasoning.md) Methodologies: method name (e.g., systematic-review.md, ablation-study.md) Findings: descriptive slug (e.g., larger-models-better-few-shot.md)

##Frontmatter

All pages must include YAML frontmatter:

Source pages also include:

Thesis pages also include:

Finding pages also include:

##Index Format

wiki/index.md lists all pages grouped by type. Each entry:

##Log Format

wiki/log.md records activity in reverse chronological order:

##Cross-referencing Rules

Use {{page-slug}} syntax to link between wiki pages Every entity and concept should appear in wiki/index.md Queries link to the sources and concepts they draw on Synthesis pages cite all contributing sources via related: Findings link back to their source via the source: frontmatter field Thesis pages reference supporting and refuting findings via related: Methodology pages are cited by the findings that used them

##Contradiction Handling

When sources contradict each other: 1. Note the contradiction in the relevant concept or entity page 2. Create or update a query page to track the open question 3. Link both sources from the query page 4. Resolve in a synthesis page once sufficient evidence exists

##Research-Specific Conventions

Keep the thesis pages updated as evidence accumulates — they are living documents Every finding should assess replication status when known Methodology pages explain the *why* (rationale) not just the *how* Distinguish between direct evidence and inference in finding pages

overview.title: Overview
overview.plainText: Overview This wiki currently tracks 175 source pages and 1602 extracted keywords. Current Coverage Variations in definitions used for describing restrictive care practices (seclusion and restraint) in adult mental health inpatient units a systematic: There is significant variation in how restrictive care practices are defined across recent literature, undermining the validity of cross-study comparisons and prevalence reporting. The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care; A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention: The article argues that current U.S. mental health law unjustly denies people with psychosocial disabilities the right to refuse treatment and access culturally responsive care, violating constitutional and international human rights protections. The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint; Prioritizing Patient Experience: The ethical analysis of chemical restraints must prioritize patient lived experience to address how psychotropic drugs undermine authentic consent and perpetuate epistemic injustice through drug-induced compliance and systemic biases like sanism. The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint Prioritizing Patient Experience: Ethical analysis of chemical restraint must prioritize patient experience and account for how psychotropic drugs alter psychological states, which can undermine authentic consent even when decisional capacity appears restored. The Limitations of Principlism: Principlism is inadequate for ethical decisions on chemical restraints because it overemphasizes individual autonomy, lacks a value theory to resolve conflicts, and ignores the collective impact on the moral community. Prevalence and variability of restrictive care practice use (physical restraint, seclusion and chemical restraint) in adult mental health inpatient settings: The pooled prevalence of physical restraint, seclusion, and chemical restraint in adult mental health inpatient settings is 14.4%, 15.8%, and 25.7%, respectively, with extreme heterogeneity (I² >99%) driven by geographical location and data collection methods. Preferences of Individual Mental Health Service Users Are Essential in Determining the Least Restrictive Type of Restraint: Ethical decisions about restraint in psychiatry must prioritize individual service users' preferences over rigid hierarchies of intervention restrictiveness. On the Relative Intrusiveness of Physical and Chemical Restraints: Consciousness-altering chemical restraints may be more intrusive on basic liberty than physical restraints because they interfere with a broader range of intentions and mental states, not just specific bodily movements. Next Areas to Develop Expand core concept pages from the most frequent keywords. Add entity, finding, thesis, and synthesis pages as patterns emerge.
overview.richText:
This wiki currently tracks 175 source pages and 1602 extracted keywords.

##Current Coverage

Variations in definitions used for describing restrictive care practices (seclusion and restraint) in adult mental health inpatient units a systematic: There is significant variation in how restrictive care practices are defined across recent literature, undermining the validity of cross-study comparisons and prevalence reporting. The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care; A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention: The article argues that current U.S. mental health law unjustly denies people with psychosocial disabilities the right to refuse treatment and access culturally responsive care, violating constitutional and international human rights protections. The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint; Prioritizing Patient Experience: The ethical analysis of chemical restraints must prioritize patient lived experience to address how psychotropic drugs undermine authentic consent and perpetuate epistemic injustice through drug-induced compliance and systemic biases like sanism. The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint Prioritizing Patient Experience: Ethical analysis of chemical restraint must prioritize patient experience and account for how psychotropic drugs alter psychological states, which can undermine authentic consent even when decisional capacity appears restored. The Limitations of Principlism: Principlism is inadequate for ethical decisions on chemical restraints because it overemphasizes individual autonomy, lacks a value theory to resolve conflicts, and ignores the collective impact on the moral community. Prevalence and variability of restrictive care practice use (physical restraint, seclusion and chemical restraint) in adult mental health inpatient settings: The pooled prevalence of physical restraint, seclusion, and chemical restraint in adult mental health inpatient settings is 14.4%, 15.8%, and 25.7%, respectively, with extreme heterogeneity (I² >99%) driven by geographical location and data collection methods. Preferences of Individual Mental Health Service Users Are Essential in Determining the Least Restrictive Type of Restraint: Ethical decisions about restraint in psychiatry must prioritize individual service users' preferences over rigid hierarchies of intervention restrictiveness. On the Relative Intrusiveness of Physical and Chemical Restraints: Consciousness-altering chemical restraints may be more intrusive on basic liberty than physical restraints because they interfere with a broader range of intentions and mental states, not just specific bodily movements.

##Next Areas to Develop

Expand core concept pages from the most frequent keywords. Add entity, finding, thesis, and synthesis pages as patterns emerge.

Sources Variations in definitions used for describing restrictive care practices (seclusion and restraint) in adult mental health inpatient units a systematic — There is significant variation in how restrictive care practices are defined across recent literature, undermining the validity of cross-study comparisons and prevalence reporting. The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care; A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention — The article argues that current U.S. mental health law unjustly denies people with psychosocial disabilities the right to refuse treatment and access culturally responsive care, violating constitutional and international human rights protections. The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint; Prioritizing Patient Experience — The ethical analysis of chemical restraints must prioritize patient lived experience to address how psychotropic drugs undermine authentic consent and perpetuate epistemic injustice through drug-induced compliance and systemic biases like sanism. The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint Prioritizing Patient Experience — Ethical analysis of chemical restraint must prioritize patient experience and account for how psychotropic drugs alter psychological states, which can undermine authentic consent even when decisional capacity appears restored. The Limitations of Principlism — Principlism is inadequate for ethical decisions on chemical restraints because it overemphasizes individual autonomy, lacks a value theory to resolve conflicts, and ignores the collective impact on the moral community. Prevalence and variability of restrictive care practice use (physical restraint, seclusion and chemical restraint) in adult mental health inpatient settings — The pooled prevalence of physical restraint, seclusion, and chemical restraint in adult mental health inpatient settings is 14.4%, 15.8%, and 25.7%, respectively, with extreme heterogeneity (I² >99%) driven by geographical location and data collection methods. Preferences of Individual Mental Health Service Users Are Essential in Determining the Least Restrictive Type of Restraint — Ethical decisions about restraint in psychiatry must prioritize individual service users' preferences over rigid hierarchies of intervention restrictiveness. On the Relative Intrusiveness of Physical and Chemical Restraints — Consciousness-altering chemical restraints may be more intrusive on basic liberty than physical restraints because they interfere with a broader range of intentions and mental states, not just specific bodily movements. Novel Approaches Needed An Experimental Study with an Alternative to Mechanical Restraint — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Machine Learning for prediction of violent behaviors in schizophrenia spectrum disorders a systematic review — Machine learning models, particularly gradient boosting, show moderate to strong predictive performance for violent behavior in schizophrenia spectrum disorders, though heterogeneity in outcomes and lack of external validation limit clinical applicability. Exploring psychiatric patient restraints Balancing safety, ethics, and patient rights in mental healthcare — The paper argues that while psychiatric restraints are sometimes necessary for safety, they pose significant risks to physical health, psychological well-being, and patient autonomy, and should be minimized through legal safeguards, de-escalation, and monitoring. Ethical Issues in Clinical Decision-Making about Involuntary Psychiatric Treatment A Scoping Review — Ethical decision-making in involuntary psychiatric treatment requires balancing autonomy with beneficence/non-maleficence/justice but is hindered by power imbalances, poor communication, and inconsistent application of criteria across jurisdictions. Ethical Dilemmas in Covert Prescribing A Case Report on Medication Administration for Noncompliant Psychiatric Patients — Covert antipsychotic administration in a schizophrenic patient with partial decision-making capacity reduced foreign object ingestion but failed to control psychosis and caused extrapyramidal symptoms, highlighting ethical tensions between autonomy and beneficence. Ethical challenges in contemporary psychiatry an overview and an appraisal of possible strategies and research needs — Psychiatry faces unique ethical challenges requiring systemic reforms centered on human rights, particularly regarding coercion, digital technologies, early intervention, and meaningful lived experience involvement. Elimination of restrictive practices from acute adult mental health care services; A qualitative evidence synthesis of the lived experience literature. — Restrictive practices in mental health are experienced as harmful, traumatic, and dehumanizing, yet persist due to perceived safety needs, lack of alternatives, and systemic gaps, requiring staff training, communication improvements, and centering lived experience for elimination. Effectiveness and Safety of Intravenous Medications for the Management of Acute Disturbance (Agitation and Other Escalating Behaviors); A Systematic Review of Prospective Interventional Studies — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Effect of coercive measures on mental health status in adult psychiatric populations a nationwide trial emulation — Exposure to any coercive measure during psychiatric hospitalization is associated with a small but statistically significant worsening of mental health at discharge, measured by a 1.91-point increase in HoNOS score. Defining chemical restraint A preliminary step towards measurement and quality assessment — The authors propose an operational definition of chemical restraint based on medication administered with intent to control behavior, avoid physical restraint/seclusion, or without consent, integrating structure, process, and intent components. Comparison of coercive practices in worldwide mental healthcare; overcoming difficulties resulting from variations in monitoring strategies — The study found 10- to 100-fold variations in coercive practices across nine high-income countries, with Japan showing exceptionally high rates despite inconsistent reporting, demonstrating that valid cross-national comparisons are possible through standardization. Comparing mental health and mental capacity law data across borders; Challenges and opportunities — Meaningful comparison of mental health and mental capacity law across jurisdictions requires harmonized data collection that includes service user experiences and social determinants, not just process metrics. Chemical Restraints and the Basic Liberties — Altering a person's conscious state (e.g., changing desires) may constitute a greater infringement on basic liberty than rendering them unconscious, challenging existing hierarchies of ethical restraint. Basic Liberties, Consent, and Chemical Restraints — Chemical restraints that sedate or undermine consciousness are more ethically problematic than those that merely alter specific conscious states because the former necessarily infringe on basic liberties. Abolition of coercion in mental health services - A European survey of feasibility — Only 31% of European mental health professionals in the FOSTREN network believe total abolition of psychiatric coercion is achievable, with skepticism driven by concerns about untreated mental illness and safety risks. When Treatment Pressures Become Coercive A Context-Sensitive Model of Informal Coercion in Mental Healthcare — Informal coercion occurs when service users justifiably believe refusing treatment will worsen their situation relative to a moral baseline and see no reasonable alternative, even without explicit threats. Wearable Technology--Another Tool in the Assessment and Treatment of Challenging Behavior Toolbox — Wearable technology can predict challenging behavior approximately three minutes prior with up to 80% accuracy but should complement, not replace, established behavioral assessment methods. Wearable Biosensing to Predict Imminent Aggressive Behavior in Psychiatric Inpatient Youths With Autism — Logistic regression achieved a mean AUC of 0.80 in predicting aggressive behavior three minutes before onset in psychiatric inpatient youth with autism using wearable biosensor data. Utilization of the Fordham Risk Screening Tool for violence risk assessment in an emergency department — The Fordham Risk Screening Tool demonstrates strong diagnostic accuracy (AUC 0.88) for violence risk assessment in emergency department settings when compared to the HCR-20 v3 criterion standard. Using coercion in mental disorders or risking the patients death An analysis of the protocols of a clinical ethics committee — Ethical decisions about coercion in psychiatry should follow a structured decision algorithm based on capacity, benefit-harm analysis, and biopsychosocial context rather than relying solely on principles or clinical judgment. Treatment without consent in adult psychiatry inpatient units a retrospective study on predictive factors — Female gender, psychotic disorder diagnosis, and involuntary admission are significant predictors of non-emergency forced medication under Swiss Civil Code Article 434, while symptom burden and aggression are not. The Role of Psychiatrists in Seclusion and Restraint Episodes a Curriculum for Psychiatric Trainees — A brief educational curriculum significantly improved psychiatry trainees' knowledge and comfort regarding seclusion and restraint, highlighting the need for ongoing training to address ethical and racial disparities. Patients' experiences with coercive mental health treatment in Flexible Assertive Community Treatment a qualitative study — Service users reported varied experiences of coercion in FACT, with safety, improved treatment quality, and participation reducing coercive experiences despite persistent concerns about forced medication and systemic distrust. Implementation of guidelines on prevention of coercion and violence (PreVCo) in psychiatry a multicentre randomised controlled trial — The PreVCo intervention significantly improved guideline adherence but did not significantly reduce the use of coercive measures in psychiatric wards. Ethical dilemmas in contemporary psychiatry; Findings from a survey of National Psychiatric Associations in Europe — National Psychiatric Associations in Europe primarily rely on international ethical codes rather than developing national ones, with strong support for collaboration under the European Psychiatric Association to build a shared database of ethical guidelines. Conflict and antagonism within global psychiatry A discourse analysis of organisational responses to the UN reports on rights-based approaches in — Organizational responses to the UN Special Rapporteur's mental health reports reveal a systemic antagonism in global psychiatry, framing psychiatric institutions as scientific authorities while portraying the Rapporteur as unscientific and ideologically driven to defend the biomedical model and resist rights-based reforms. Chemical restraint as behavioural euthanasia; case studies from the Royal Commission into Aged Care Quality and Safety — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. A descriptive study on the use of restrictive interventions for potentially or actually violent patients in the emergency department — Restrictive interventions were used in 70% of potentially or actually violent ED patients, with intoxication, high triage acuity, and involuntary status being the strongest predictors—not clinical agitation due to missing data. Who’s the Boss Ethical Dilemmas in the Treatment of Children and Adolescents — Ethical psychiatric care for minors requires balancing adolescent assent, parental authority, confidentiality, and legal obligations, particularly in evolving contexts like technology use and family dynamics. Use of a Novel Standardized Administration Protocol Reduces Agitation Pro Re Nata (PRN) Medication Requirements The Birmingham Agitation Management (BAM) — Implementation of the Birmingham Agitation Management (BAM) protocol reduced PRN medication administration by 42.5% without increasing seclusion or restraint rates in an inpatient psychiatric setting. The Utilization of a Rapid Agitation Scale and Treatment Protocol for Patient and Staff Safety in an Inpatient Psychiatric Setting — Implementation of the EMR-integrated Modified Agitation Severity Scale (MASS) protocol reduced restraint duration by 44% and was sustained in clinical use without increasing nurse burnout. The Conditions for Ethical Chemical Restraints — Ethical chemical restraints must minimally restrict liberty, be medically appropriate, involve informed consent (or emergency exception), and for non-consciousness-affecting agents, must promote the patient's rational capacity post-intervention. Seclusion or Restraint — Seclusion or restraint should be used only as a last resort, guided by patient-centered, trauma-informed, and culturally competent principles, with restraint avoided due to higher risks and patient preference for seclusion. Patients’ and staff members’ experiences of restrictive practices in acute mental health in-patient settings; systematic review and thematic synthesis — Restrictive practices in acute psychiatric inpatient settings are experienced as overwhelmingly negative by both patients and staff, causing psychological harm and damaging relationships despite being viewed by staff as sometimes unavoidable. Mental health and the law What else is needed for particularly vulnerable contexts facing armed conflict and development obstacles — Existing international legal instruments fail to protect mental health rights in low-resource and conflict-affected contexts, necessitating new legal frameworks that balance autonomy with protection needs. Law and psychiatry--current and future perspectives — The relationship between law and psychiatry is bidirectional and shaped by evolving societal values, particularly patient autonomy, rather than a unidirectional legal dominance over medical practice. Impact of diagnosis on outcomes for compulsory treatment orders in New Zealand — Community Treatment Orders reduce psychiatric admissions only for psychotic disorders while increasing admissions and depot antipsychotic use across all other diagnostic groups, revealing diagnosis-specific outcome signatures. Human rights in mental healthcare; A review of current global situation — Despite policy advancements like the CRPD and WHO QualityRights, significant gaps remain in the real-world implementation of human rights in mental health services due to stigma, coercive practices, and reliance on substitute over supported decision-making. Ethical and Legal Concerns Associated With Withdrawing Mechanical Circulatory Support; A U.S. Perspective — Withdrawing mechanical circulatory support at the request of an informed patient or surrogate is ethically and legally permissible in the U.S. and does not constitute physician-assisted suicide or euthanasia when the intent is to remove burdensome treatment. Effectiveness of De-Escalation in Reducing Aggression and Coercion in Acute Psychiatric Units. A Cluster Randomized Study — De-escalation training significantly reduced aggression (73%), severe aggression (86%), and aggression-related physical restraint use (70%) in psychiatric units across Slovenia. Current trends in restrictive interventions in psychiatry; a European perspective — The use of coercive measures in European psychiatric inpatient settings remains widespread but highly variable, with significant human rights concerns, yet may be necessary as a last resort for safety. Association of surveillance technology and staff opinions with physical restraint use in nursing homes Cross-sectional study — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Approval of Coercion in Psychiatry in Public Perception and the Role of Stigmatization — Public approval of psychiatric coercion is driven by perceived dangerousness and treatment usefulness, with stigmatizing attitudes gaining influence as coercive measures become more severe. Which Emergent Medication Should I Give Next Repeated Use of Emergent Medications to Treat Acute Agitation — The paper highlights a critical gap in guidelines for managing persistent acute agitation after first-line medication failure and proposes risk-informed, stepwise clinical algorithms for sequential medication use to minimize polypharmacy and adverse events. The WPA Code of Ethics for Psychiatry — The World Psychiatric Association adopted its first comprehensive Code of Ethics for Psychiatry in October 2020, structured around five adapted medical ethics principles and designed as a living document to guide clinical practice, education, research, and public mental health advocacy globally. Prescription patterns in psychiatric compulsory care polypharmacy and high-dose antipsychotics — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Mental health professionals feelings and attitudes towards coercion — Despite acknowledging coercion as a rights violation, many mental health professionals still view it as indispensable and beneficial, with attitudes structured around four dimensions: internal pressure, emotional impact, external pressure, and relational involvement. Human Rights and Adherence to Treatment in Patients with Severe Mental Illness; Mental Healthcare Act 2017 Perspective — There is a positive correlation between medication adherence and human rights awareness in the independent admission group, and the supported admission group shows significantly lower fulfillment of emotional needs under the Mental Healthcare Act 2017. Definition and Measurement of Physical and Chemical Restraint in Long-Term Care; A Systematic Review — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Coercive Measures in Psychiatry A Review of Ethical Arguments — Coercion in psychiatry is ethically permissible only in exceptional circumstances when it serves higher values (e.g., long-term autonomy, safety) after a transparent, balanced evaluation of pros and cons. Clinical Utility of Wearable Sensors and Patient-Reported Surveys in Patients With Schizophrenia Noninterventional, Observational Study — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Chemical restraint; A qualitative synthesis review of adult service user and staff experiences in mental health settings — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Applying Human Rights and Reducing Coercion in Psychiatry following Service User-Led Education; A Qualitative Study. — Service user-led education shifts psychiatry residents' attitudes toward human rights and recovery-oriented practice but fails to translate into sustained behavioral change due to systemic barriers like powerlessness and hierarchical training models. The Role of the Psychiatrist in Seclusion and Restraint — Psychiatrists should take a more active leadership role in reducing seclusion and restraint through the Six Core Strategies framework, moving beyond reactive ordering to preventive, team-based care. The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care: A Human The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care; A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention — The article argues that U.S. mental health law unjustly violates the bodily autonomy of people with psychosocial disabilities by permitting coercive treatment while denying access to voluntary, culturally responsive care, violating domestic and international human rights law. Structured assessments for imminent aggression in mental health and correctional settings Systematic review and meta-analysis — The Brøset Violence Checklist and Dynamic Appraisal of Situational Aggression demonstrate large predictive effect sizes for imminent (24-hour) aggression and are broadly equivalent in performance despite variations in study quality and settings. Psychiatric coercion and violence ethical, legal and preventive aspects — Psychiatric patients face widespread coercion and violence in hospitals due to inadequate legal frameworks, unclear clinical criteria, and ignored bioethics, leading to rights violations and poor therapeutic outcomes. Prevalence of the use of chemical restraint in the management of challenging behaviours associated with adult mental health conditions A — Chemical restraint is used in 7.4% of all admissions to psychiatric and emergency settings globally, with a significant declining trend over time but persistent high use among restrained individuals (43.1%). Prevalence and variability in use of physical and chemical restraints in residential aged care facilities; A systematic review and meta-analysis — The pooled prevalence of physical and chemical restraint use in residential aged care facilities is 33% and 32%, respectively, with substantial variability explained by measurement approach and geographic region. Physical and Pharmacological Restraints in Hospital Care; Protocol for a Systematic Review — This protocol outlines a systematic review to address the lack of evidence on the benefits and harms of physical and pharmacological restraints in hospital settings. Physical and Chemical Restraints (an Update) — The update emphasizes de-escalation as first-line, details risks and protocols for physical and chemical restraints, and underscores documentation, training, and regulatory compliance for safe use in emergency departments. Mental Health as a Basic Human Right and the Interference of Commercialized Science — A rights-based approach to mental health is necessary to counteract commercialized science, which prioritizes industry interests over social justice by obscuring social determinants through biomedical reductionism and industry influence on research, guidelines, and education. Is Coercive Treatment Necessary or a Method of Mass Control — Coercive treatment, while sometimes necessary for safety, has historically and systematically functioned as a tool of racialized social control that perpetuates intergenerational trauma. Implementing Alternatives to Coercion; A Key Component of Improving Mental Health Care — Coercion in mental health care is overused globally and violates fundamental human rights; implementing evidence-based alternatives is essential to improving care quality and upholding dignity. Experiences with legally mandated treatment in patients with schizophrenia A systematic review of qualitative studies — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Ethical Principles in Psychiatric Practice; A Systematic Review — Ethical principles in psychiatric practice are context-dependent and vary across cultural, religious, economic, and political settings rather than being universally applicable. Defensive practice is indefensible how defensive medicine runs counter to the ethical and professional obligations of clinicians — Defensive medicine is ethically indefensible because it violates core medical ethical principles by prioritizing clinician self-interest over patient welfare through deception, harm without benefit, and erosion of trust. An introduction to clinical ethics in psychiatry — Ethical decision-making in psychiatry requires navigating ambiguous principles, identity complexities, and relational dynamics, emphasizing dialogue and reflection over rigid application of ethical models. A wicked problem Chemical restraint towards a definition — The term "chemical restraint" suffers from definitional ambiguity due to interchangeable use with related terms like rapid tranquilization and therapeutic sedation, hindering clinical practice, research, and policy development. A Human Rights-Based Approach to Psychiatry; Is It Possible — The pilot project in Region Västra Götaland demonstrated that a human rights-based approach can reduce coercive measures in psychiatric care while fostering patient and staff empowerment and influencing regional policy toward a 'zero vision' for coercion. Treatment Options for Acute Agitation in Psychiatric Patients Theoretical and Empirical Evidence — Haloperidol alone or combined with anticholinergics like promethazine is often preferred for acute agitation due to extensive evidence, while benzodiazepines alone are less effective and carry higher risks despite common use. The effectiveness of chemical restraint in managing acute agitation and aggression; A systematic review of randomized controlled trials — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. The Criminalisation of Coercive Control; The Power of Law — Criminalizing coercive control fails victims because it misunderstands the coercive nature of law itself and fails to see victims' lived experiences holistically. Surveying the Geneva impasse; Coercive care and human rights — The Geneva Impasse stems from a disagreement over whether CRPD Article 14 establishes an absolute right against disability-based liberty deprivation, prohibiting coercive care under any circumstances. Psychiatric Advance Directives Under the Convention on the Rights of Persons With Disabilities Why Advance Instructions Should Be Able to — The radical interpretation of the CRPD by the UN Committee undermines Psychiatric Advance Directives' effectiveness during mental health crises by prioritizing current preferences over advance instructions when they conflict. Predicting Inpatient Aggression in Forensic Services Using Remote Monitoring Technology Qualitative Study of Staff Perspectives — Staff perceived passive remote monitoring as potentially useful for early aggression detection but identified significant barriers including safety risks, privacy concerns, workload impact, and user engagement challenges. Mental health and human rights in the 21st century — Involuntary psychiatric interventions generally violate international human rights law under the CRPD, and mental health must shift to a rights-based model centered on will and preferences to eliminate coercion. International research into 22 years of use of chemical restraint An evidence overview — Over 22 years, 311 studies on chemical restraint revealed persistent focus on medication efficacy and safety, limited methodological innovation, and recurring ethical tensions between efficacy, safety, and patient dignity. How right is right-based mental health law — India's Mental Healthcare Act 2017 aligns with the UNCRPD in promoting a rights-based approach but faces challenges due to conceptual tensions in the convention, particularly regarding legal capacity and the lack of attention to mental health-specific vulnerability. Staff Training Reduces the Use of Physical Restraint in Mental Health Service, Evidence-based Reflection for China — Staff training interventions significantly reduce the use of physical restraint in mental health settings, supporting evidence-based improvements in patient safety and care quality. Restraint in Psychiatric Patients; A Literature Review — Restraint effectively reduces violent behavior in psychiatric patients but poses significant physical and psychological risks, requiring strict adherence to evidence-based guidelines and comprehensive documentation to minimize harm. Responding to the UN Special Rapporteur's anti-psychiatry bias — The UN Special Rapporteur's report on mental health exhibits anti-psychiatry bias by unfairly blaming biomedical psychiatry for human rights abuses while ignoring systemic treatment gaps and evidence of treatment efficacy in severe cases. An integrative review exploring the physical and psychological harm inherent in using restraint in mental health inpatient settings — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. The UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities 'Rights, will and preferences' in relation to mental health disabilities — The CRPD's requirement to respect "rights, will and preferences" necessitates distinguishing enduring will from transient preferences, where honoring prior will may justify overriding current preferences without constituting impermissible substitute decision-making. Public attitudes towards involuntary admission and treatment by mental health services in Norway — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Protocol for the management of psychiatric patients with psychomotor agitation — The protocol provides standardized, locally adapted guidance for managing psychomotor agitation in psychiatric patients, emphasizing early detection and least invasive interventions to reduce coercive measures. Physical Restraint A Historical Review and Current Practice — The use of physical restraint in psychiatric care has shifted from a historically justified practice to a highly regulated last-resort intervention due to patient rights advocacy, landmark litigation, and documented harms such as asphyxiation-related deaths. On the concept of restraint in psychiatry — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Measurement of agitation and aggression in adult and aged neuropsychiatric patients review of definitions and frequently used measurement scales — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Key international themes in coercion — Coercion in mental healthcare is globally pervasive but varies by economic context, taking legalized forms in high-income countries and extreme, unregulated practices like pasung in low/middle-income countries. Ethics of Coercive Treatment and Misuse of Psychiatry — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Ethical dilemmas in psychiatry; When teams disagree — Ethical decision-making in mental health teams is driven by emotions and social dynamics rather than pure reason, and resolving disagreement requires reflective practice and social persuasion to align intuitions with ethical justification. Effects of Intramuscular Midazolam and Lorazepam on Acute Agitation in Non-Elderly Subjects - A Systematic Review — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Proportionality and Absolute Rights — Absolute rights derive their absoluteness not from lacking limitation clauses or from predictable balancing outcomes, but from specifying duties of non-action between persons, which makes them immune to proportionality balancing. Paternalismivi. autonomy - are we barking up the wrong tree — The authors argue that efforts to reduce medical paternalism by legally enforcing patient autonomy are misguided because paternalism is a relational issue requiring dynamic balancing of ethical principles, not merely correcting an ethical hierarchy. Ethics and medical practice; Why psychiatry is unique — Psychiatry presents unique ethical challenges requiring standards beyond general medical ethics due to patient vulnerability, stigma, and the intimate nature of disclosures. Chemical and Physical Restraint of Patients — Nurses demonstrate moderate overall knowledge of physical and chemical restraint with significant gaps in legal and ethical understanding despite strong beliefs in safety benefits. Coercion and trust in psychiatry the ultimate contradiction — Coercion in psychiatry is fundamentally incompatible with trust, genuine care, and human rights, constituting an ultimate contradiction. ‘Rapid tranquillisation’; an historical perspective on its emergence in the context of the development of antipsychotic medications — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Human Rights Violations and Mental Illness — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. The Mysore Declaration — The Mysore Declaration establishes a framework to define and reduce coercive measures in Indian mental health care by integrating human rights principles with contextual factors like family involvement and legal reform. Coercion in psychiatric care Can paternalism justify coercion — Paternalism's assumptions of incompetence and imperfectly justify psychiatric coercion, while dangerousness fails to justify any form of coercion, and impairment only justifies experienced coercive measures like restraint or forced medication. Advance Directives A Tool for Reducing Coercion — Advance directives can reduce coercion in mental health care by enabling prior consent and aligning crisis interventions with previously expressed patient preferences. Is psychiatry torn in different ethical directions — Psychiatry is uniquely ethically conflicted because it operates under legislation that is predominantly utilitarian (Mental Health Act 2007), while other relevant laws (Human Rights Act 1998, Mental Capacity Act 2005) are rights-focused, creating a split not seen in other medical specialties. Can we justify eliminating coercive measures in psychiatry — Appeals to autonomy and human dignity alone are insufficient to justify eliminating coercive measures in psychiatry, as these principles can be interpreted to either support or oppose seclusion depending on interpretation and context. Can we justify eliminating coercive measures in psychiatry — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Right of Everyone to the Enjoyment of the Highest Attainable Standard of Physical and Mental Health, Paul Hunt — Mental health must be reframed through a human rights lens, moving beyond the biomedical model to uphold legal capacity, informed consent, and social inclusion as core components of equitable care. The Controversy over “Chemical Restraint” in Acute Care Psychiatry — The article argues that chemical restraint lacks a clear definition and is often conflated with treatment, despite regulatory efforts to distinguish based on therapeutic intent, with significant use in psychiatric emergencies and growing reliance on atypical antipsychotics amid ongoing ethical and clinical ambiguity. The Psychiatrist's Double Bind; The Right to Refuse Medication — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. The constitutional right to refuse antipsychotic medications. — Involuntarily hospitalized mental patients possess a qualified constitutional right to refuse antipsychotic medication based on privacy rights, overridable only upon findings of incompetence or dangerousness. The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 22 — Mechanical restraint can be ethically and clinically justified in high-secure forensic psychiatric settings as a last resort for life-threatening self-injury or extreme violence when used within a robust legal and governance framework. The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 21 — Coercive measures in forensic psychiatric care should be minimized through primary prevention strategies and staff training, though they remain necessary as last resorts in extreme situations. The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 20 — Coercive measures in psychiatric care are harmful and ethically indefensible from a patient perspective, driven by systemic issues like understaffing and cost-cutting rather than clinical necessity. The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 18 — Patient experiences of coercive measures in psychiatric care are complex and not uniformly negative, with some reporting feelings of safety or benefit when accompanied by respectful communication and therapeutic relationships. The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 17 — Healthcare workers in high-security psychiatric hospitals experience role conflict between caregiving and security enforcement, leading to emotional detachment and desensitization as coping mechanisms when using coercive measures. The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 16 — Staff attitudes toward coercive measures in forensic psychiatry are shaped by perceived efficacy and views of aggression as patient-intent-driven, rather than objective safety or patient dignity. The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 08 — Coercion in mental health care extends beyond formal detention to include informal admissions, community treatment, and subtle pressures, with rising use across the care pathway in England/Wales despite goals to reduce it. The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 07 — Coercive practices vary widely across countries, with no universally least restrictive option, and banning one method often increases use of others while causing measurable patient distress and dignity violations. The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 04 — Coercion in psychiatric care is embedded in institutional structure and social processes, not limited to specific interventions, shaping patient experience through loss of autonomy and identity. The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 03 — Involuntary psychiatric treatment and commitment must comply with proportionality, necessity, and procedural safeguards under international human rights law to avoid violating rights to liberty and privacy. The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 02 — Forensic psychiatric patients remain excluded from human rights advancements despite conditions violating international law, and the CRPD and CAT offer a framework to address this neglect. Psychiatry and the Law - 07 — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Psychiatry and the Law - 06 — Civil commitment requires "clear and convincing" evidence of mental illness and dangerousness or grave disability, balancing liberty interests against treatment needs as established in Addington v. Texas. Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -07 — Article 12 of the CRPD should be interpreted through indivisibility and dignity to allow both supported decision-making and substitute decision-making as a last resort for those with severe cognitive impairment. Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -06 — Article 12's requirement of legal capacity "on an equal basis with others" must be interpreted through inclusive equality, allowing supported decision-making as norm and substitute decision-making as last resort to uphold dignity and substantive equality. Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -05 — The chapter argues that dignity in the CRPD is an inherent legal principle comprising five dimensions—equal worth, autonomy for the capable, interdependence, embodiment, and indivisibility of rights—that guides interpretation of rights like Article 12 on legal capacity. Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -04 — The chapter argues that autonomy under CRPD Article 12 is best understood as an achievement requiring both internal competencies and external support, integrating civil-political and socio-economic rights through the indivisibility principle. Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -03 — The principle of indivisibility demands an interpretation of Article 12 of the CRPD that allows for, and may require, substitute decision-making as a last resort to promote equality and social inclusion for some persons with cognitive disability. Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -02 — The chapter argues that effective disability rights frameworks must balance social model insights with recognition of embodied impairment, particularly for cognitive disability, to avoid undermining autonomy. Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -01 — Article 12 of the CRPD requires both supported decision-making and, as a last resort, substitute decision-making in 'hard cases' to uphold the indivisible nature of human rights, including dignity and safety. Is Involuntary Placement and Non-Consensual Treatment Ever Compliant with UN Human Rights Standards_ — There is no unified UN position on whether involuntary placement and non-consensual treatment comply with human rights standards, as divergent views exist across UN bodies ranging from abolitionist stances to conditional permissibility under strict safeguards. Human Rights in Psychiatry - 17 — Abolishing psychiatric coercion under the UN CRPD creates systemic dilemmas in forensic, suicide, and dementia care that require legal, healthcare, and social reforms beyond psychiatry alone. Human Rights in Psychiatry - 15 — Human rights-based psychosocial support requires prioritizing service user preferences over clinical evidence, fully respecting legal capacity, and eliminating coercion except for self-binding advance directives. Human Rights in Psychiatry - 08 — Psychiatric coercion fails to benefit most patients, is not consistently used as a last resort, shows limited therapeutic effectiveness, and lacks evidence for restoring autonomy. Human Rights in Psychiatry - 06 — The justification of coercion in psychiatry has evolved from social control to clinical-ethical frameworks but remains ethically and legally contested due to conflicting values like autonomy versus beneficence. Human Rights in Psychiatry - 05 — The UN CRPD challenges psychiatric coercion by asserting that legal capacity must not be denied based on mental health status, requiring supported decision-making over substitute decision-making. Human Rights in Psychiatry - 03 — Psychiatric coercion violates human rights and fails to benefit individuals, as evidenced by rising global rates, diagnostic uncertainty, and disregard for patient preferences in housing and employment. Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 22 — Voluntariness in research consent depends on whether influences are external, intentional, illegitimate, and causally effective, not merely on the presence of pressure or incentives. Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 21 — Ethical research on coercion in psychiatry is essential and feasible when prioritizing ongoing assessment of decisional capacity and voluntarism, though challenges remain in ensuring truly voluntary participation among vulnerable populations. Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 19 — Patients under court-ordered assisted outpatient treatment do not report significantly higher perceived coercion than those in voluntary treatment, challenging assumptions that legal mandates increase subjective feelings of coercion. Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 18 — The German psychiatric system maintained coercive practices for seventy years through biological reductionism, despite the existence of humane alternatives like Soteria and need-adapted treatment. Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 13 — The acceptability and experience of coercive psychiatric treatments are shaped by cultural context, with traditional societies often viewing such measures as socially acceptable when aligned with family and religious values, contrasting with Western emphasis on individual autonomy. Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 11 — Summary generated but key finding not captured yet. Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 09 — Coercion is an inherent and defining element of psychiatry, legitimized only when balanced against individual rights and public safety through legal and ethical constraints. Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 07 — De-escalation techniques based on emotional regulation, active listening, and relationship-building can prevent aggression and reduce coercive interventions in psychiatric settings by addressing underlying needs and emotions before violence escalates. Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 06 — It is not currently possible to define objective, universally applicable best practice standards for coercive treatment in psychiatry due to the limitations of virtue ethics, evidence-based medicine, and consensus approaches when applied in isolation. Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 05 — Mandated community treatment uses leverage from housing, justice, and welfare systems—not just hospitalization—to influence treatment adherence, with outcomes varying by mechanism and implementation quality. Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 04 — Stigma of mental illness contributes to the frequency and acceptability of coercive measures, and the experience of coercive measures reinforces stigma and negative public perceptions of psychiatry. Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 03 — Person-centred psychiatry offers a framework for addressing coercion by centering the person, fostering dialogue, and strengthening clinician-patient relationships grounded in empathy and mutual respect. Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 18 — Alternatives to coercion in mental health—such as trauma-informed care, peer support, and shared decision-making—reduce coercion by centering patient autonomy, dignity, and therapeutic relationships across inpatient, community, and treatment-wide settings. Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 12 — True safety and security in mental health settings requires balancing procedural, environmental, and relational dimensions, with relational safety being key to mutual well-being of service users and staff. Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 11 — Ward design significantly influences aggression and coercive measures in psychiatric settings, with healing environment principles reducing seclusion and aggression through environmental modifications like privacy, natural light, and improved staff visibility. Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 10 — Despite widespread ratification of the CRPD, involuntary admission persists globally, but evidence shows psychiatric advance directives and joint crisis plans can reduce coercive admissions by prioritizing patient autonomy and advance planning. Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 09 — The UN CRPD challenges psychiatric coercion by asserting that persons with psychosocial disabilities retain legal capacity and must consent to all medical interventions, rejecting traditional justifications based on impaired judgment. Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 08 — Coercion in mental healthcare encompasses both formal (legally regulated, physical force) and informal (psychological pressure) forms, and its justification depends on context—via the harm principle or soft paternalism—subject to strict conditions like necessity, proportionality, and procedural justice, though some argue it can never be justified under human rights frameworks. Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 07 — Violence risk assessment must integrate static and dynamic risk factors, protective factors, and staff/environmental factors to effectively prevent violence through structured, collaborative approaches like the Early Recognition Method. Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 06 — Psychiatric settings have the highest healthcare workplace violence rates, with measurement complicated by dual perspectives and significant under-reporting, while exposure shows dose-response relationships with serious health consequences like increased suicide risk. Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 05 — International efforts to reduce coercion in mental health are hindered by inconsistent definitions and measurement, despite consensus on core concepts and growing human rights frameworks. Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 04 — Stakeholders differ significantly in their views on coercion: service users experience it as a rights violation and trauma, while staff and informal caregivers often justify it as necessary for safety despite recognizing its harm to relationships and dignity. Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 03 — Coercion in mental health is culturally shaped, with Western contexts emphasizing individual autonomy and Eastern contexts emphasizing family decision-making, impacting patient experience and therapeutic relationships despite universal human rights frameworks like the UNCRPD. Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 02 — Violence and abuse by staff in UK mental health settings, while not representative of most staff, occurs with concerning frequency and is systematically underreported and inadequately addressed due to systemic failures, power imbalances, and societal stigma. Clinical Manual of Psychiatry and Law - 09 — The Tarasoff decision established that psychiatrists have a legal duty to protect identifiable third parties from foreseeable violence by patients, though actual liability remains rare due to predictive uncertainty and jurisdictional variation. Clinical Manual of Psychiatry and Law - 06 — Seclusion and restraint are legally permissible only as a last resort to prevent imminent harm, subject to stringent federal, state, and accreditation regulations requiring due process, timely review, and continuous monitoring. Clinical Manual of Psychiatry and Law - 05 — Psychiatric malpractice liability arises when treatment deviates from the professional standard of care, but adhering to sound clinical practice generally aligns with effective risk management. Clinical Manual of Psychiatry and Law - 04 — Informed consent requires competency, information disclosure, and voluntariness, with competency assessed functionally rather than by diagnosis, and the right to refuse treatment being qualified and subject to state-specific legal standards and substituted consent for incapacitated patients. Clinical Handbook of Psychiatry and Law - 03 — The right to treatment ensures only minimally adequate care for involuntarily committed patients, not optimal or chosen treatment, and its real-world impact is limited by judicial reluctance to enforce funding and systemic shifts toward community-based care. Clinical Handbook of Psychiatry and Law - 02 — Psychiatric emergency evaluation requires balancing clinical judgment with legal duties, where involuntary hospitalization is justified primarily by police powers (danger to self/others) rather than parens patriae, and must comply with procedural standards and least restrictive principles. Queries (None yet) Comparisons (None yet) Findings (None yet) Methodology (None yet) Synthesis (None yet) Thesis (None yet)
