social-determinants-of-health.title: social determinants of health
social-determinants-of-health.plainText: social determinants of health Why It Matters Social determinants are essential for understanding mental health beyond biomedical reductionism They reveal how commercialized science obscures structural inequities in research and policy Including social determinants enables meaningful cross-jurisdictional comparisons of mental health laws Addressing them is necessary for rights-based approaches to mental health care Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Rights-based approach | Mental health must be understood as a basic human right, requiring attention to social determinants to counter commercialized science | 2020 - Cosgrove, shaughnessy - Mental Health as a Basic Human Right and the Interference of Commercialized Science | | Data harmonization | Cross-border comparisons of mental health laws require inclusion of social determinants and service user experiences, not just process metrics | 2024 - Davidson et al. - Comparing mental health and mental capacity law data across borders; Challenges and opportunities | Sub-topics None Related links human-rights-based-approach social-model-of-disability biopsychosocial-model person-centered-care service-user-involvement recovery-oriented-care trauma-informed-care structural-competency — not in list, omitted health-equity — not in list, omitted Open questions How can social determinants be operationalized in mental health law data collection across jurisdictions? How do power dynamics in knowledge production marginalize social determinant frameworks in favor of biomedical models? What role do commercial interests play in shaping what counts as valid evidence in mental health policy?

voluntariness.title: voluntariness
voluntariness.plainText: voluntariness Why It Matters Central to informed consent and ethical decision-making in psychiatric treatment Determines whether consent is legally valid and ethically permissible Distinguishes legitimate medical interventions from coercive or unethical practices Critical for protecting vulnerable populations from undue influence in research and treatment contexts Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |----------|------------|-------------| | Ethical research standards | Voluntariness requires ongoing assessment of decisional capacity and voluntarism; ethical research is feasible when prioritized, despite challenges with vulnerable populations | Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 21 | | Informed consent framework | Voluntariness is a core component of informed consent, alongside competency and information disclosure; competency is assessed functionally, not by diagnosis | Clinical Manual of Psychiatry and Law - 04 | | Determinants of voluntariness | Voluntariness depends on whether influences are external, intentional, illegitimate, and causally effective—not merely on the presence of pressure or incentives | Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 22 | Sub-topics informed-consent Related concepts autonomy, bodily-autonomy, patient-autonomy, decisional-capacity, coercion, coercion-in-psychiatry, coercive-treatment, ethical-decision-making Open questions How can voluntariness be operationalized and measured consistently across clinical and research settings? What safeguards are necessary to ensure voluntariness among populations with fluctuating decisional capacity? How do cultural and systemic factors influence perceptions of voluntariness in psychiatric contexts? What legal standards best balance individual autonomy with state interests in substituted consent scenarios?
voluntariness.richText:
##Why It Matters

Central to informed consent and ethical decision-making in psychiatric treatment Determines whether consent is legally valid and ethically permissible Distinguishes legitimate medical interventions from coercive or unethical practices Critical for protecting vulnerable populations from undue influence in research and treatment contexts ##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |----------|------------|-------------| | Ethical research standards | Voluntariness requires ongoing assessment of decisional capacity and voluntarism; ethical research is feasible when prioritized, despite challenges with vulnerable populations | {{Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 21}} | | Informed consent framework | Voluntariness is a core component of informed consent, alongside competency and information disclosure; competency is assessed functionally, not by diagnosis | {{Clinical Manual of Psychiatry and Law - 04}} | | Determinants of voluntariness | Voluntariness depends on whether influences are external, intentional, illegitimate, and causally effective—not merely on the presence of pressure or incentives | {{Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 22}} | ##Sub-topics

{{informed-consent}} ##Related concepts

{{autonomy}}, {{bodily-autonomy}}, {{patient-autonomy}}, {{decisional-capacity}}, {{coercion}}, {{coercion-in-psychiatry}}, {{coercive-treatment}}, {{ethical-decision-making}} ##Open questions

How can voluntariness be operationalized and measured consistently across clinical and research settings? What safeguards are necessary to ensure voluntariness among populations with fluctuating decisional capacity? How do cultural and systemic factors influence perceptions of voluntariness in psychiatric contexts? What legal standards best balance individual autonomy with state interests in substituted consent scenarios?

extrapyramidal-side-effects-eps.title: Extrapyramidal side effects (EPS)
extrapyramidal-side-effects-eps.plainText: Extrapyramidal side effects (EPS) Why It Matters Critical safety concern when using antipsychotics for acute agitation management Influences medication selection in psychiatric emergency protocols Directly impacts patient comfort, treatment adherence, and risk of discontinuation Must be weighed against efficacy when evaluating pharmacological interventions for disturbance Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | EPS as a limiting factor in acute agitation treatment | Extrapyramidal side effects reduce the tolerability and safety profile of certain antipsychotics used for rapid tranquilization, necessitating careful agent selection | 2024 - Castro et al. - Effectiveness and Safety of Intravenous Medications for the Management of Acute Disturbance (Agitation and Other Escalating Behaviors); A Systematic Review of Prospective Interventional Studies | | Benzodiazepines as preferred alternative to avoid EPS | Midazolam and lorazepam demonstrate efficacy in acute agitation without inducing EPS, making them favorable first-line options in non-elderly populations | 2017 - Kousgaard et al. - Effects of Intramuscular Midazolam and Lorazepam on Acute Agitation in Non-Elderly Subjects - A Systematic Review | | Need for EPS monitoring in pharmacologic intervention protocols | Safety assessments for acute disturbance management must include systematic evaluation of motor side effects to prevent iatrogenic harm | 2024 - Castro et al. - Effectiveness and Safety of Intravenous Medications for the Management of Acute Disturbance (Agitation and Other Escalating Behaviors); A Systematic Review of Prospective Interventional Studies | Sub-topics extrapyramidal-symptoms tardive-dyskinesia Related concepts antipsychotics benzodiazepines chemical-restraint rapid-tranquilization rapid-tranquillisation patient-safety adverse-effects medication-safety Open questions How do specific dosing regimens of antipsychotics influence the incidence and severity of EPS in acute agitation settings? Are there identifiable patient phenotypes more susceptible to developing EPS during short-term antipsychotic use for agitation? What are the comparative EPS risks between intramuscular and intravenous routes of antipsychotic administration in emergency contexts? Can prophylactic agents mitigate EPS without compromising the anti-agitation efficacy of primary treatments?

six-core-strategies.title: Six Core Strategies
six-core-strategies.plainText: Six Core Strategies What It Is The Six Core Strategies is a framework designed to reduce the use of seclusion and restraint in mental health settings. It emphasizes leadership, data use, workforce development, prevention tools, service user involvement, and debriefing to promote safer, more therapeutic environments. Its application supports systemic change by addressing both organizational practices and relational dynamics in inpatient care. Role in the Literature In 2018 - Cusack et al. - An integrative review exploring the physical and psychological harm inherent in using restraint in mental health inpatient settings: Referenced as a foundational framework linked to Safewards, informing practice implications for restraint reduction. In 2020 - Rodrigues - Implementing Alternatives to Coercion; A Key Component of Improving Mental Health Care: Described as a structured approach guiding implementation through leadership, data use, workforce development, prevention tools, service user roles, and debriefing. In 2024 - Savage et al. - Comparison of coercive practices in worldwide mental healthcare; overcoming difficulties resulting from variations in monitoring strategies: Cited as an evidence-based framework supporting debriefing, leadership involvement, and data-driven change to reduce coercion globally. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 12: Identified as one of three models helping institutions enhance relational safety by targeting leadership, data, workforce, prevention, patient roles, and debriefing.
six-core-strategies.richText:
##What It Is

The Six Core Strategies is a framework designed to reduce the use of seclusion and restraint in mental health settings. It emphasizes leadership, data use, workforce development, prevention tools, service user involvement, and debriefing to promote safer, more therapeutic environments. Its application supports systemic change by addressing both organizational practices and relational dynamics in inpatient care.

##Role in the Literature

In {{2018 - Cusack et al. - An integrative review exploring the physical and psychological harm inherent in using restraint in mental health inpatient settings}}: Referenced as a foundational framework linked to Safewards, informing practice implications for restraint reduction. In {{2020 - Rodrigues - Implementing Alternatives to Coercion; A Key Component of Improving Mental Health Care}}: Described as a structured approach guiding implementation through leadership, data use, workforce development, prevention tools, service user roles, and debriefing. In {{2024 - Savage et al. - Comparison of coercive practices in worldwide mental healthcare; overcoming difficulties resulting from variations in monitoring strategies}}: Cited as an evidence-based framework supporting debriefing, leadership involvement, and data-driven change to reduce coercion globally. In {{Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 12}}: Identified as one of three models helping institutions enhance relational safety by targeting leadership, data, workforce, prevention, patient roles, and debriefing.

parens-patriae.title: Parens Patriae
parens-patriae.plainText: Parens Patriae Why It Matters Underpins legal authority to make decisions for vulnerable individuals who lack decision-making capacity Central to debates on involuntary treatment and civil commitment in psychiatry Balances state protection with individual rights, especially in cases of mental incapacity Shapes ethical and legal frameworks for surrogate decision-making in medical contexts Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | State as protector | The state has a duty to act as parent for individuals unable to care for themselves due to mental incapacity | 2016 - Sidhu, Srinivasraghavan - Ethics and medical practice; Why psychiatry is unique | | Limits to paternalism | Parens patriae must be constrained by proportionality and respect for dignity to avoid overreach | 2016 - Sidhu, Srinivasraghavan - Ethics and medical practice; Why psychiatry is unique | | Tension with autonomy | Application risks undermining personal liberty when used to justify involuntary interventions | 1980 - Ford - The Psychiatrist's Double Bind; The Right to Refuse Medication | Sub-topics substitute-decision-making best-interest-standard substituted-judgment Related concepts autonomy beneficence paternalism involuntary-treatment civil-commitment capacity-assessment informed-consent least-restrictive-alternative human-rights legal-capacity Open questions How should parens patriae be balanced against emerging supported decision-making models under CRPD Article 12? What safeguards prevent misuse of parens patriae to justify coercion without sufficient evidence of incapacity? Can parens patriae be reconciled with recovery-oriented approaches that emphasize autonomy and self-determination? How do cultural variations affect the acceptability and application of parens patriae in mental health law?
parens-patriae.richText:
##Why It Matters

Underpins legal authority to make decisions for vulnerable individuals who lack decision-making capacity Central to debates on involuntary treatment and civil commitment in psychiatry Balances state protection with individual rights, especially in cases of mental incapacity Shapes ethical and legal frameworks for surrogate decision-making in medical contexts

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | State as protector | The state has a duty to act as parent for individuals unable to care for themselves due to mental incapacity | {{2016 - Sidhu, Srinivasraghavan - Ethics and medical practice; Why psychiatry is unique}} | | Limits to paternalism | Parens patriae must be constrained by proportionality and respect for dignity to avoid overreach | {{2016 - Sidhu, Srinivasraghavan - Ethics and medical practice; Why psychiatry is unique}} | | Tension with autonomy | Application risks undermining personal liberty when used to justify involuntary interventions | {{1980 - Ford - The Psychiatrist's Double Bind; The Right to Refuse Medication}} |

##Open questions

How should parens patriae be balanced against emerging supported decision-making models under CRPD Article 12? What safeguards prevent misuse of parens patriae to justify coercion without sufficient evidence of incapacity? Can parens patriae be reconciled with recovery-oriented approaches that emphasize autonomy and self-determination? How do cultural variations affect the acceptability and application of parens patriae in mental health law?

informed-consent.title: Informed Consent
informed-consent.plainText: Informed Consent Why It Matters Central to respecting patient autonomy and decision-making capacity in psychiatric care Balances therapeutic intervention with legal and ethical rights to refuse treatment Highlights tension between clinical beneficence and patient self-determination Underpins ethical frameworks for involuntary treatment and coercion debates Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---|---|---| | Ethical decision-making requires dialogue | Clinical ethics in psychiatry emphasizes relational dynamics and reflection over rigid models | 2020 - Adshead, Cave - An introduction to clinical ethics in psychiatry | | Right to refuse medication is fundamental | Patients possess a fundamental right to decline psychiatric medication | 1980 - Ford - The Psychiatrist's Double Bind; The Right to Refuse Medication | Sub-topics advance-directives advance-directives capacity-assessment shared-decision-making substituted-decision-making therapeutic-misconception voluntariness Related concepts autonomy beneficence coercion coercion-in-psychiatry clinical-ethics confidentiality due-process human-rights patient-autonomy patient-centered-care person-centered-care relational-autonomy therapeutic-relationship Open questions How can informed consent be meaningfully obtained in emergency psychiatric situations? What constitutes valid consent when decision-making capacity is fluctuating or impaired? How do cultural and relational factors influence the validity of consent processes? What safeguards prevent coercion from undermining the voluntariness of consent?

last-resort-principle.title: last resort principle
last-resort-principle.plainText: last resort principle Why It Matters Determines legal and ethical boundaries for coercive interventions in psychiatric care Serves as a safeguard against abuse of power in involuntary treatment settings Central to compliance with human rights frameworks and clinical accreditation standards Balances patient safety with preservation of autonomy and dignity Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Legally permissible as last resort | Seclusion and restraint allowed only to prevent imminent harm, subject to strict oversight | Clinical Manual of Psychiatry and Law - 06 | | No unified UN stance on compliance | UN bodies diverge: some reject involuntary treatment outright, others allow it only under strict safeguards | Is Involuntary Placement and Non-Consensual Treatment Ever Compliant with UN Human Rights Standards_ | Sub-topics least-restrictive-principle involuntary-treatment seclusion chemical-restraints mechanical-restraint forced-medication involuntary-medication coercive-treatment involuntary-commitment psychiatric-restraint Related concepts human-rights patient-autonomy informed-consent beneficence non-maleficence least-restrictive-alternative de-escalation patient-safety involuntary-hospitalization convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with-disabilities Open questions How can the "last resort" threshold be objectively measured across diverse clinical and jurisdictional contexts? What safeguards are sufficient to ensure that last-resort interventions do not become routine or coercive in practice? Is there a viable path toward global consensus on whether involuntary treatment can ever comply with human rights standards? How do cultural and systemic biases influence the application of the last resort principle in real-world settings?
last-resort-principle.richText:
##Why It Matters

Determines legal and ethical boundaries for coercive interventions in psychiatric care Serves as a safeguard against abuse of power in involuntary treatment settings Central to compliance with human rights frameworks and clinical accreditation standards Balances patient safety with preservation of autonomy and dignity

sanism.title: sanism
sanism.plainText: sanism Why It Matters Reveals systemic bias in psychiatric practices that undermines the validity of consent for people with psychosocial disabilities Explains how coercive treatments like chemical restraint perpetuate injustice by dismissing lived experience Connects to human rights violations under CRPD and international law regarding autonomy and bodily integrity Central to critiques of psychiatric power structures that prioritize compliance over person-centered care Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Sanism as structural injustice | Sanism operates as a pervasive bias that invalidates the perspectives and autonomy of people with psychosocial disabilities, particularly in psychiatric settings | 2024 - Dougherty et al. - The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint; Prioritizing Patient Experience | | Sanism undermines informed consent | Drug-induced compliance and epistemic injustice caused by psychotropic drugs are manifestations of sanism that compromise authentic consent | 2024 - Dougherty et al. - The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint; Prioritizing Patient Experience | | Sanism violates human rights | Coercive psychiatric practices rooted in sanism breach domestic and international human rights law, including CRPD and constitutional protections | 2020 - Bergan - The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care: A Human The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care; A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention, 2024 - Bergan - The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care; A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention | | Sanism excludes marginalized voices | Forensic psychiatric patients face compounded exclusion due to sanism, preventing their inclusion in human rights advancements despite CRPD and CAT frameworks | The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 02 | Sub-topics chemical-restraint coercive-treatment informed-consent patient-experience Related concepts epistemic-injustice autonomy bodily-autonomy human-rights convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with-disabilities chemical-restraints coercion-in-psychiatry patient-safety Open questions How can psychiatric systems measure and counteract sanism in clinical decision-making beyond individual bias training? What role does sanism play in the persistent use of chemical restraint despite evidence of harm and lack of therapeutic benefit? In what ways do current legal standards for competence and consent fail to account for sanism-induced epistemic distortion? How might supported decision-making models be adapted to resist sanist assumptions about incapacity in psychiatric contexts?
sanism.richText:
##Why It Matters

Reveals systemic bias in psychiatric practices that undermines the validity of consent for people with psychosocial disabilities Explains how coercive treatments like chemical restraint perpetuate injustice by dismissing lived experience Connects to human rights violations under CRPD and international law regarding autonomy and bodily integrity Central to critiques of psychiatric power structures that prioritize compliance over person-centered care

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Sanism as structural injustice | Sanism operates as a pervasive bias that invalidates the perspectives and autonomy of people with psychosocial disabilities, particularly in psychiatric settings | {{2024 - Dougherty et al. - The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint; Prioritizing Patient Experience}} | | Sanism undermines informed consent | Drug-induced compliance and epistemic injustice caused by psychotropic drugs are manifestations of sanism that compromise authentic consent | {{2024 - Dougherty et al. - The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint; Prioritizing Patient Experience}} | | Sanism violates human rights | Coercive psychiatric practices rooted in sanism breach domestic and international human rights law, including CRPD and constitutional protections | {{2020 - Bergan - The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care: A Human The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care; A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention}}, {{2024 - Bergan - The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care; A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention}} | | Sanism excludes marginalized voices | Forensic psychiatric patients face compounded exclusion due to sanism, preventing their inclusion in human rights advancements despite CRPD and CAT frameworks | {{The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 02}} |

##Sub-topics

{{chemical-restraint}} {{coercive-treatment}} {{informed-consent}} {{patient-experience}}

##Related concepts

##Open questions

How can psychiatric systems measure and counteract sanism in clinical decision-making beyond individual bias training? What role does sanism play in the persistent use of chemical restraint despite evidence of harm and lack of therapeutic benefit? In what ways do current legal standards for competence and consent fail to account for sanism-induced epistemic distortion? How might supported decision-making models be adapted to resist sanist assumptions about incapacity in psychiatric contexts?

cms-regulations.title: CMS regulations
cms-regulations.plainText: CMS regulations Why It Matters Central to legal and ethical boundaries in psychiatric care Governs permissible use of restraint and seclusion Requires compliance with federal oversight and due process Shapes clinical decision-making during behavioral emergencies Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Legal compliance | Seclusion and restraint are legally permissible only as a last resort, subject to federal, state, and accreditation regulations | Clinical Manual of Psychiatry and Law - 06 | | Clinical practice shift | Physical restraint has become a highly regulated last-resort intervention due to patient rights advocacy and documented harms | 2017 - Masters - Physical Restraint A Historical Review and Current Practice | | Preventive leadership | Psychiatrists should lead reduction efforts through the Six Core Strategies, moving beyond reactive orders to team-based prevention | 2020 - Masters et al. - The Role of the Psychiatrist in Seclusion and Restraint | Sub-topics centers-for-medicare-and-medicaid-services-cms physical-restraint seclusion-and-restraint six-core-strategies last-resort-principle due-process Related concepts patient-safety informed-consent patient-autonomy least-restrictive-alternative coercion-in-psychiatry mental-health-law therapeutic-milieu trauma-informed-care Open questions How can CMS regulations be consistently applied across diverse clinical settings? What barriers prevent full adoption of preventive strategies despite regulatory guidance? How do state-level variations affect uniformity in restraint and seclusion practices? What metrics best evaluate compliance and patient outcomes under current CMS standards?

biopsychosocial-model.title: biopsychosocial model
biopsychosocial-model.plainText: biopsychosocial model Why It Matters Provides a framework for integrating biological, psychological, and social factors in mental health assessment and intervention Challenges purely biomedical approaches by emphasizing context, autonomy, and social determinants of health Supports ethical decision-making in coercive practices by requiring consideration of patient context beyond clinical symptoms Aligns with human rights frameworks that demand holistic, person-centered care in mental health services Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Ethical use of coercion requires biopsychosocial assessment | Coercion decisions should follow structured algorithms incorporating capacity, benefit-harm, and biopsychosocial context, not just clinical judgment or principles | 2023 - Steinert - Using coercion in mental disorders or risking the patients death An analysis of the protocols of a clinical ethics committee | | Mental health care must move beyond biomedical model | Mental health should be reframed through a human rights lens, with legal capacity, informed consent, and social inclusion as core components of equitable care | 2005 - Hunt - Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Right of Everyone to the Enjoyment of the Highest Attainable Standard of Physical and Mental Health, Paul Hunt | Sub-topics No specific sub-topics from the source material map directly to existing concept articles in the list. Related concepts biomedical-model capacity-assessment coercion coercion-in-psychiatry informed-consent legal-capacity human-rights human-rights-based-approach person-centered-care recovery-model social-determinants-of-health trauma-informed-care Open questions How can biopsychosocial assessment be standardized and implemented consistently across diverse clinical settings, especially under time constraints? What metrics or tools exist to evaluate whether biopsychosocial considerations are meaningfully influencing coercion decisions in practice? How do power imbalances between clinicians and patients affect the authenticity of biopsychosocial engagement in mental health encounters? In what ways might overreliance on biopsychosocial framing inadvertently justify coercion by appearing more "humane" while preserving coercive practices?
biopsychosocial-model.richText:

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Ethical use of coercion requires biopsychosocial assessment | Coercion decisions should follow structured algorithms incorporating capacity, benefit-harm, and biopsychosocial context, not just clinical judgment or principles | {{2023 - Steinert - Using coercion in mental disorders or risking the patients death An analysis of the protocols of a clinical ethics committee}} | | Mental health care must move beyond biomedical model | Mental health should be reframed through a human rights lens, with legal capacity, informed consent, and social inclusion as core components of equitable care | {{2005 - Hunt - Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Right of Everyone to the Enjoyment of the Highest Attainable Standard of Physical and Mental Health, Paul Hunt}} |

convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with.title: Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities
convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with.plainText: Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Why It Matters Centralizes legal capacity and decision-making rights in disability rights discourse Challenges biomedical models of mental health by centering human rights and autonomy Sparks debate over compatibility of involuntary treatment with international human rights law Drives global shifts toward supported decision-making and coercion reduction in psychiatry Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Abolitionist stance | Involuntary psychiatric interventions violate CRPD and must be eliminated | 2019 - Puras et al. - Mental health and human rights in the 21st century | | Conditional permissibility | Involuntary treatment may be allowed under strict safeguards as last resort | Is Involuntary Placement and Non-Consensual Treatment Ever Compliant with UN Human Rights Standards_ | | Supported decision-making priority | CRPD requires supported decision-making; advance directives overridden by current wishes | 2019 - Scholten et al. - Psychiatric Advance Directives Under the Convention on the Rights of Persons With Disabilities Why Advance Instructions Should Be Able to | | Pluralistic model of dignity | Dignity in CRPD includes five dimensions: equal worth, autonomy, interdependence, embodiment, indivisibility | Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -05 | | Indivisibility allows substitute decision-making | Article 12 permits substitute decision-making as last resort for severe cognitive impairment | Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -03, Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -07 | | Systemic barriers limit reform | Service user education fails to change practice due to hierarchy and powerlessness | 2021 - Every‐Palmer et al. - Applying Human Rights and Reducing Coercion in Psychiatry following Service User-Led Education; A Qualitative Study. | | Inadequate protection in crisis contexts | Existing laws fail in low-resource and conflict zones; need new frameworks | 2022 - Miller - Mental health and the law What else is needed for particularly vulnerable contexts facing armed conflict and development obstacles | | Medical model inadequate | Mental health must be reframed through human rights lens, not biomedical model | 2005 - Hunt - Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Right of Everyone to the Enjoyment of the Highest Attainable Standard of Physical and Mental Health, Paul Hunt | | Balance model for cognitive disability | Effective frameworks must recognize embodied impairment to avoid undermining autonomy | Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -02 | Sub-topics supported-decision-making legal-capacity substitute-decision-making psychiatric-advance-directives article-12 principle-of-indivisibility five-dimensional-dignity Related concepts autonomy human-rights coercion informed-consent best-interest-standard last-resort-principle least-restrictive-alternative deinstitutionalization disability-rights recovery-model person-centered-care social-model-of-disability biopsychosocial-model biomedical-model legal-reform human-rights-based-approach therapeutic-jurisprudence involuntary-treatment compulsory-treatment guardianship Open questions How can Article 12 be interpreted to respect both advance directives and current wishes during mental health crises? What safeguards are necessary if substitute decision-making is permitted as a last resort under the CRPD? How can CRPD implementation be strengthened in low-resource and conflict-affected settings? Why does rights-based education often fail to produce sustained changes in psychiatric practice?
convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with.richText:
##Why It Matters

Centralizes legal capacity and decision-making rights in disability rights discourse Challenges biomedical models of mental health by centering human rights and autonomy Sparks debate over compatibility of involuntary treatment with international human rights law Drives global shifts toward supported decision-making and coercion reduction in psychiatry

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Abolitionist stance | Involuntary psychiatric interventions violate CRPD and must be eliminated | {{2019 - Puras et al. - Mental health and human rights in the 21st century}} | | Conditional permissibility | Involuntary treatment may be allowed under strict safeguards as last resort | {{Is Involuntary Placement and Non-Consensual Treatment Ever Compliant with UN Human Rights Standards_}} | | Supported decision-making priority | CRPD requires supported decision-making; advance directives overridden by current wishes | {{2019 - Scholten et al. - Psychiatric Advance Directives Under the Convention on the Rights of Persons With Disabilities Why Advance Instructions Should Be Able to}} | | Pluralistic model of dignity | Dignity in CRPD includes five dimensions: equal worth, autonomy, interdependence, embodiment, indivisibility | {{Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -05}} | | Indivisibility allows substitute decision-making | Article 12 permits substitute decision-making as last resort for severe cognitive impairment | {{Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -03}}, {{Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -07}} | | Systemic barriers limit reform | Service user education fails to change practice due to hierarchy and powerlessness | {{2021 - Every‐Palmer et al. - Applying Human Rights and Reducing Coercion in Psychiatry following Service User-Led Education; A Qualitative Study.}} | | Inadequate protection in crisis contexts | Existing laws fail in low-resource and conflict zones; need new frameworks | {{2022 - Miller - Mental health and the law What else is needed for particularly vulnerable contexts facing armed conflict and development obstacles}} | | Medical model inadequate | Mental health must be reframed through human rights lens, not biomedical model | {{2005 - Hunt - Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Right of Everyone to the Enjoyment of the Highest Attainable Standard of Physical and Mental Health, Paul Hunt}} | | Balance model for cognitive disability | Effective frameworks must recognize embodied impairment to avoid undermining autonomy | {{Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -02}} |

##Sub-topics

##Related concepts

##Open questions

How can Article 12 be interpreted to respect both advance directives and current wishes during mental health crises? What safeguards are necessary if substitute decision-making is permitted as a last resort under the CRPD? How can CRPD implementation be strengthened in low-resource and conflict-affected settings? Why does rights-based education often fail to produce sustained changes in psychiatric practice?

safewards.title: Safewards
safewards.plainText: Safewards What It Is Safewards is an evidence-based model designed to reduce conflict and containment in psychiatric inpatient settings through staff training, environmental modifications, and relational safety interventions. It promotes alternatives to restrictive practices by enhancing safety and communication between patients and staff. The model matters in mental health literature as a recommended approach to minimizing coercion and improving care quality. Role in the Literature In 2018 - Cusack et al. - An integrative review exploring the physical and psychological harm inherent in using restraint in mental health inpatient settings: Model promoted to reduce restrictive practices; cited as a recommended alternative. In 2020 - Muir-Cochrane - A wicked problem Chemical restraint towards a definition: referenced as an evidence-based, least restrictive approach to reducing coercive practices. In 2020 - Rodrigues - Implementing Alternatives to Coercion; A Key Component of Improving Mental Health Care: An evidence-based model designed to reduce conflict and containment in psychiatric wards through staff training and environmental modifications. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 12: presented as another model promoting relational safety through specific interventions like mutual help, clear boundaries, and de-escalation to reduce conflict and containment in mental health wards.
safewards.richText:
##What It Is

Safewards is an evidence-based model designed to reduce conflict and containment in psychiatric inpatient settings through staff training, environmental modifications, and relational safety interventions. It promotes alternatives to restrictive practices by enhancing safety and communication between patients and staff. The model matters in mental health literature as a recommended approach to minimizing coercion and improving care quality.

##Role in the Literature

In {{2018 - Cusack et al. - An integrative review exploring the physical and psychological harm inherent in using restraint in mental health inpatient settings}}: Model promoted to reduce restrictive practices; cited as a recommended alternative. In {{2020 - Muir-Cochrane - A wicked problem Chemical restraint towards a definition}}: referenced as an evidence-based, least restrictive approach to reducing coercive practices. In {{2020 - Rodrigues - Implementing Alternatives to Coercion; A Key Component of Improving Mental Health Care}}: An evidence-based model designed to reduce conflict and containment in psychiatric wards through staff training and environmental modifications. In {{Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 12}}: presented as another model promoting relational safety through specific interventions like mutual help, clear boundaries, and de-escalation to reduce conflict and containment in mental health wards.

un-crpd.title: UN-CRPD
un-crpd.plainText: UN-CRPD Why It Matters Central to legal and ethical frameworks in psychiatry concerning patient autonomy and consent Shapes debates on legal capacity, supported decision-making, and coercive treatment Informs human rights-based approaches to mental health law and policy Drives discourse on the shift from substitute to supported decision-making models Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | UN-CRPD as transformative framework | Establishes equal legal capacity and mandates supported decision-making, rejecting substitute decision-making regimes | 2017 - Steinert - Ethics of Coercive Treatment and Misuse of Psychiatry, 2022 - Steinert et al. - Law and psychiatry--current and future perspectives | | Bidirectional law-psychiatry relationship | Law and psychiatry influence each other, with evolving societal values—especially patient autonomy—shaping their interaction | 2022 - Steinert et al. - Law and psychiatry--current and future perspectives | Sub-topics convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with-disabilities crpd crpd-article-12 supported-decision-making legal-capacity substitute-decision-making substituted-decision-making substituted-judgment person-centered-care patient-autonomy Related concepts autonomy basic-liberty beneficence bodily-autonomy coercion coercion-in-psychiatry coercive-treatment human-rights human-rights-based-approach informed-consent legal-reform mental-health-law patient-centered-care relational-autonomy supported-decision-making substitute-decision-making UN convention on the rights of persons with disabilities Open questions How can supported decision-making be operationalized in psychiatric crisis contexts without compromising safety? What mechanisms ensure accountability when implementing CRPD-consistent laws across diverse legal systems? How does the UN-CRPD reconcile with existing mental health laws that permit involuntary treatment under certain conditions? What role should psychiatry play in advancing CRPD implementation while maintaining clinical legitimacy?
un-crpd.richText:
##Why It Matters

Central to legal and ethical frameworks in psychiatry concerning patient autonomy and consent Shapes debates on legal capacity, supported decision-making, and coercive treatment Informs human rights-based approaches to mental health law and policy Drives discourse on the shift from substitute to supported decision-making models

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | UN-CRPD as transformative framework | Establishes equal legal capacity and mandates supported decision-making, rejecting substitute decision-making regimes | {{2017 - Steinert - Ethics of Coercive Treatment and Misuse of Psychiatry}}, {{2022 - Steinert et al. - Law and psychiatry--current and future perspectives}} | | Bidirectional law-psychiatry relationship | Law and psychiatry influence each other, with evolving societal values—especially patient autonomy—shaping their interaction | {{2022 - Steinert et al. - Law and psychiatry--current and future perspectives}} |

physical-restraint.richText:
##Why It Matters

A restrictive practice with significant ethical, legal, and clinical implications in psychiatric care Central to debates on patient safety, autonomy, and human rights in acute psychiatric settings Frequently studied in relation to de-escalation and coercive interventions Its reduction is a key indicator of quality improvement in mental health services

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Supports reduction via de-escalation | De-escalation training significantly reduces physical restraint use | {{2022 - Celofiga et al. - Effectiveness of De-Escalation in Reducing Aggression and Coercion in Acute Psychiatric Units. A Cluster Randomized Study}} | | Focuses on conceptual and ethical dimensions | Restraint raises fundamental questions about liberty, coercion, and psychiatric power | {{2017 - Negroni - On the concept of restraint in psychiatry}} |

##Open questions

How can de-escalation strategies be optimally implemented and sustained across diverse psychiatric settings to further reduce reliance on physical restraint? What are the long-term psychological and relational impacts of physical restraint on patients, particularly in terms of trust and therapeutic alliance? How do cultural, institutional, and legal contexts shape the acceptability and use of physical restraint as a coercive measure in mental health care? To what extent do alternatives to restraint (e.g., sensory modulation, peer support, advanced directives) achieve comparable safety outcomes without compromising patient autonomy?

legal-capacity.title: Legal Capacity
legal-capacity.plainText: Legal Capacity Why It Matters Central to disability rights and autonomy under international human rights law Highlights tension between supported decision-making and substitute decision-making Reflects evolving legal standards for cognitive disability and embodied impairment Addresses dignity, safety, and indivisibility of human rights in care contexts Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Supported decision-making as primary | Article 12 of CRPD requires supported decision-making to uphold dignity and rights | Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -01 | | Substitute decision-making as last resort | Substitute decision-making only permissible in 'hard cases' as a last resort | Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -01 | | Balance of social model and embodied impairment | Effective frameworks must recognize cognitive disability while avoiding autonomy erosion | Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -02 | Sub-topics supported-decision-making substitute-decision-making decision-making-capacity legal-reform human-rights-based-approach Related concepts autonomy disability-rights dignity crpd crpd-article-12 best-interest-standard substituted-judgment person-centered-care informed-consent legal-capacity Open questions How can supported decision-making be effectively implemented across diverse legal and cultural contexts? What safeguards prevent substitute decision-making from becoming a default rather than a last resort? How do we balance recognition of cognitive impairment with the presumption of legal capacity? What role does dignity play in determining the limits of autonomy in mental health care?
legal-capacity.richText:
##Why It Matters

Central to disability rights and autonomy under international human rights law Highlights tension between supported decision-making and substitute decision-making Reflects evolving legal standards for cognitive disability and embodied impairment Addresses dignity, safety, and indivisibility of human rights in care contexts

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Supported decision-making as primary | Article 12 of CRPD requires supported decision-making to uphold dignity and rights | {{Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -01}} | | Substitute decision-making as last resort | Substitute decision-making only permissible in 'hard cases' as a last resort | {{Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -01}} | | Balance of social model and embodied impairment | Effective frameworks must recognize cognitive disability while avoiding autonomy erosion | {{Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -02}} |

##Sub-topics

{{supported-decision-making}} {{substitute-decision-making}} {{decision-making-capacity}} {{legal-reform}} {{human-rights-based-approach}}

##Related concepts

{{autonomy}} {{disability-rights}} dignity crpd {{crpd-article-12}} {{best-interest-standard}} {{substituted-judgment}} {{person-centered-care}} {{informed-consent}} {{legal-capacity}}

##Open questions

How can supported decision-making be effectively implemented across diverse legal and cultural contexts? What safeguards prevent substitute decision-making from becoming a default rather than a last resort? How do we balance recognition of cognitive impairment with the presumption of legal capacity? What role does dignity play in determining the limits of autonomy in mental health care?

heterogeneity.title: heterogeneity
heterogeneity.plainText: heterogeneity Why It Matters Reveals inconsistencies in measuring and reporting restrictive practices across settings Highlights how geography and methodology influence prevalence estimates Challenges assumptions about uniformity in care practices globally Underscores need for standardized measurement in research and policy Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Measurement variability drives heterogeneity | Differences in assessment tools and definitions explain much of the variation in restraint use | 2020 - Lee et al. - Prevalence and variability in use of physical and chemical restraints in residential aged care facilities; A systematic review and meta-analysis | | Geographic differences explain heterogeneity | Regional variations in policy, culture, and practice account for disparities in restrictive care use | 2020 - Lee et al. - Prevalence and variability in use of physical and chemical restraints in residential aged care facilities; A systematic review and meta-analysis, 2024 - Belayneh et al. - Prevalence and variability of restrictive care practice use (physical restraint, seclusion and chemical restraint) in adult mental health inpatient settings | | Extreme heterogeneity in mental health settings | I² >99% indicates near-total inconsistency in reported restrictive practices, largely due to geography and data methods | 2024 - Belayneh et al. - Prevalence and variability of restrictive care practice use (physical restraint, seclusion and chemical restraint) in adult mental health inpatient settings | Sub-topics prevalence systematic-review meta-analysis meta-regression Related concepts physical-restraint chemical-restraint seclusion restrictive-interventions coercion involuntary-treatment patient-safety human-rights ethical-decision-making least-restrictive-principle Open questions How can measurement tools be standardized to reduce heterogeneity in restrictive practice research? What role do unmeasured cultural or institutional factors play in observed geographic variation? Are current prevalence estimates reliable given extreme heterogeneity (I² >99%) in mental health settings? How might differences in legal frameworks across regions contribute to heterogeneity in restrictive practices?
heterogeneity.richText:
##Why It Matters

Reveals inconsistencies in measuring and reporting restrictive practices across settings Highlights how geography and methodology influence prevalence estimates Challenges assumptions about uniformity in care practices globally Underscores need for standardized measurement in research and policy

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Measurement variability drives heterogeneity | Differences in assessment tools and definitions explain much of the variation in restraint use | {{2020 - Lee et al. - Prevalence and variability in use of physical and chemical restraints in residential aged care facilities; A systematic review and meta-analysis}} | | Geographic differences explain heterogeneity | Regional variations in policy, culture, and practice account for disparities in restrictive care use | {{2020 - Lee et al. - Prevalence and variability in use of physical and chemical restraints in residential aged care facilities; A systematic review and meta-analysis}}, {{2024 - Belayneh et al. - Prevalence and variability of restrictive care practice use (physical restraint, seclusion and chemical restraint) in adult mental health inpatient settings}} | | Extreme heterogeneity in mental health settings | I² >99% indicates near-total inconsistency in reported restrictive practices, largely due to geography and data methods | {{2024 - Belayneh et al. - Prevalence and variability of restrictive care practice use (physical restraint, seclusion and chemical restraint) in adult mental health inpatient settings}} |

chemical-restraint.title: Chemical restraint
chemical-restraint.plainText: Chemical restraint Why It Matters Central to ethical and legal debates about autonomy, consent, and coercion in psychiatric and aged care settings Frequently overlaps with broader discussions of restraint, forced medication, and human rights protections Subject to inconsistent definitions and measurement across jurisdictions and research Highlighted in international frameworks (e.g., CRPD) as a practice requiring reduction and oversight Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|-----------|------------| | Clinical effectiveness in acute settings | Chemical restraint can rapidly manage acute agitation and aggression, particularly in emergency psychiatry | 2019 - Muir‐Cochrane et al. - The effectiveness of chemical restraint in managing acute agitation and aggression; A systematic review of randomized controlled trials | | Experiential harms and power dynamics | Service users and staff report psychological trauma, loss of dignity, and perceived coercion, even when clinically indicated | 2021 - Muir‐Cochrane, Oster - Chemical restraint; A qualitative synthesis review of adult service user and staff experiences in mental health settings | | Overuse and polypharmacy risks | Routine use in compulsory care settings correlates with high-dose antipsychotics and polypharmacy, raising safety concerns | 2021 - Kaikoushi et al. - Prescription patterns in psychiatric compulsory care polypharmacy and high-dose antipsychotics | | Systemic critique in aged care | Chemical restraint may function as a form of "behavioural euthanasia" in aged care, reflecting systemic neglect and coercion | 2023 - Cain et al. - Chemical restraint as behavioural euthanasia; case studies from the Royal Commission into Aged Care Quality and Safety | | Conceptual and operational inconsistency | Inconsistent definitions and measurement hinder international efforts to reduce coercion despite human rights consensus | Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 05 | | Philosophical challenge to autonomy | Challenges notions of liberty and consent, engaging debates on paternalism, beneficence, and legal capacity | 2017 - Negroni - On the concept of restraint in psychiatry | Sub-topics chemical-restraints coercive-measures-in-psychiatry forced-medication involuntary-medication physical-restraints restraint seclusion-and-restraint Related concepts autonomy beneficence coercion human-rights informed-consent least-restrictive-principle patient-safety proportionality-principle therapeutic-intent trauma-informed-care Open questions How can clinical efficacy be balanced with service user autonomy and trauma minimization in real-world settings? What standardized definitions and metrics could enable cross-jurisdictional comparison and accountability? To what extent do current practices reflect systemic ableism or ageism rather than clinical necessity? How might supported decision-making and advance directives reduce reliance on chemical restraint in crisis care?
chemical-restraint.richText:
##Why It Matters

Central to ethical and legal debates about autonomy, consent, and coercion in psychiatric and aged care settings Frequently overlaps with broader discussions of restraint, forced medication, and human rights protections Subject to inconsistent definitions and measurement across jurisdictions and research Highlighted in international frameworks (e.g., CRPD) as a practice requiring reduction and oversight

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|-----------|------------| | Clinical effectiveness in acute settings | Chemical restraint can rapidly manage acute agitation and aggression, particularly in emergency psychiatry | {{2019 - Muir‐Cochrane et al. - The effectiveness of chemical restraint in managing acute agitation and aggression; A systematic review of randomized controlled trials}} | | Experiential harms and power dynamics | Service users and staff report psychological trauma, loss of dignity, and perceived coercion, even when clinically indicated | {{2021 - Muir‐Cochrane, Oster - Chemical restraint; A qualitative synthesis review of adult service user and staff experiences in mental health settings}} | | Overuse and polypharmacy risks | Routine use in compulsory care settings correlates with high-dose antipsychotics and polypharmacy, raising safety concerns | {{2021 - Kaikoushi et al. - Prescription patterns in psychiatric compulsory care polypharmacy and high-dose antipsychotics}} | | Systemic critique in aged care | Chemical restraint may function as a form of "behavioural euthanasia" in aged care, reflecting systemic neglect and coercion | {{2023 - Cain et al. - Chemical restraint as behavioural euthanasia; case studies from the Royal Commission into Aged Care Quality and Safety}} | | Conceptual and operational inconsistency | Inconsistent definitions and measurement hinder international efforts to reduce coercion despite human rights consensus | {{Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 05}} | | Philosophical challenge to autonomy | Challenges notions of liberty and consent, engaging debates on paternalism, beneficence, and legal capacity | {{2017 - Negroni - On the concept of restraint in psychiatry}} |

mechanical-restraint.title: mechanical-restraint
mechanical-restraint.plainText: # Mechanical restraint ## Why It Matters - A key coercive practice in psychiatric settings with significant ethical and legal implications - Central to debates on reducing coercion in mental health care despite inconsistent definitions - Intersects with human rights frameworks and legal standards for patient autonomy and dignity - Represents a focal point for reform efforts aimed at minimizing restrictive interventions ## Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---|---|---| | Critical stance | Inconsistent definitions and measurement hinder global efforts to reduce coercion in mental health | Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 05 | | Reform-oriented | Novel approaches are needed to replace or reduce reliance on mechanical restraint | 2024 - Steinert et al. - Novel Approaches Needed An Experimental Study with an Alternative to Mechanical Restraint | ## Sub-topics - physical-restraint - psychiatric-restraint - coercive-measures - seclusion-and-restraint - restraint ## Related concepts - coercion - coercion-in-psychiatry - chemical-restraint - seclusion - forced-medication - involuntary-treatment - least-restrictive-alternative - patient-autonomy - human-rights - trauma-informed-care ## Open questions - How can mechanical restraint be consistently defined and measured across different jurisdictions and care settings? - What are the most effective and ethically acceptable alternatives to mechanical restraint in managing acute agitation? - To what extent do current human rights frameworks successfully reduce the use of mechanical restraint in practice? - How do cultural and institutional factors influence the persistence of mechanical restraint despite reform efforts?

coercion.title: Coercion
coercion.plainText: Coercion Why It Matters Central to ethical and legal debates in mental health care due to its impact on human rights Inconsistent definitions hinder global efforts to reduce coercive practices Core to discussions on autonomy, consent, and patient safety in psychiatric settings Linked to systemic issues like institutional betrayal and racial disparities in care Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Definition inconsistency | Lack of standardized definition impedes measurement and cross-study comparison | Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 05 | | Need for innovation | Experimental alternatives to mechanical restraint are necessary to reduce harm | 2024 - Steinert et al. - Novel Approaches Needed An Experimental Study with an Alternative to Mechanical Restraint | | Human rights alignment | Growing consensus that coercion should align with CRPD and other human rights frameworks | Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 05 | | Measurement gap | Despite ethical agreement, reliable tools for assessing coercion remain underdeveloped | Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 05 | Sub-topics coercion-in-psychiatry coercive-measures coercive-treatment formal-coercion informal-coercion mechanical-restraint chemical-restraint physical-restraint forced-medication involuntary-treatment Related concepts autonomy informed-consent patient-autonomy supported-decision-making substituted-decision-making legal-capacity beneficence non-maleficence human-rights therapeutic-relationship de-escalation least-restrictive-principle last-resort-principle seclusion restraint risk-assessment trauma-informed-care recovery-oriented-care cultural-responsiveness Open questions How can coercion be consistently defined and measured across diverse mental health systems and cultural contexts? human-rights.title: human rights
human-rights.plainText: human rights Why It Matters Central to ethical debates on psychiatric care, particularly regarding coercion and capacity Framed as essential for equitable mental health treatment and protection against abuse Central to international legal frameworks like the CRPD, shaping domestic policy and practice Highlights tensions between biomedical models and rights-based approaches in psychiatry Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Human rights as essential framework for mental health | Mental health must be reframed through a human rights lens, prioritizing legal capacity, informed consent, and social inclusion | 2005 - Hunt - Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Right of Everyone to the Enjoyment of the Highest Attainable Standard of Physical and Mental Health, Paul Hunt | | Critique of paternalism in psychiatric coercion | Paternalism unjustifiably assumes incompetence; dangerousness does not justify coercion; impairment only justifies extreme measures as last resort | 2012 - Seo et al. - Coercion in psychiatric care Can paternalism justify coercion | | Global variability in coercion practices | Coercion is widespread but takes legalized forms in high-income countries and extreme, unregulated forms (e.g., pasung) in low/middle-income countries | 2017 - Molodynski et al. - Key international themes in coercion | | Defense of psychiatry against anti-psychiatry bias | UN Special Rapporteur's report unfairly blames biomedical psychiatry while ignoring treatment efficacy and systemic gaps | 2018 - Dharmawardene et al. - Responding to the UN Special Rapporteur's anti-psychiatry bias | | Reliance on international ethical standards | European psychiatric associations favor international codes over national ones, supporting shared guidelines via EPA | 2023 - Samochowiec et al. - Ethical dilemmas in contemporary psychiatry; Findings from a survey of National Psychiatric Associations in Europe | | Need for systemic reform centered on human rights | Ethical challenges in psychiatry require reform focused on coercion, digital tech, early intervention, and lived experience involvement | 2024 - Galderisi et al. - Ethical challenges in contemporary psychiatry an overview and an appraisal of possible strategies and research needs | | CRPD Article 12 supports both supported and substitute decision-making | Article 12 should be interpreted via indivisibility and dignity to allow supported decision-making as primary, substitute as last resort | Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -07 | Sub-topics legal-capacity supported-decision-making substitute-decision-making human-rights-based-approach convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with-disabilities crpd-article-12 coercion-in-psychiatry informed-consent autonomy dignity Related concepts antipsychotics antipsychotics basic-liberty beneficence biomedical-model bodily-autonomy civil-commitment conflict-of-interest de-institutionalization disability-rights ethical-decision-making ethics forced-medication guardianship human-dignity involuntary-treatment least-restrictive-alternative non-maleficence patient-autonomy patient-safety principle-of-indivisibility psychiatric-ethics recovery-model relational-autonomy restrictive-interventions rights-based-ethics sanism seclusion substituted-judgment trauma-informed-care voluntariness Open questions How can human rights frameworks be operationalized in clinical practice without undermining clinical judgment or safety? What is the appropriate balance between supported decision-making and substitute decision-making under CRPD Article 12 in cases of severe cognitive impairment? To what extent does biomedical psychiatry contribute to human rights violations, versus systemic underfunding and social determinants? How should emerging technologies (e.g., digital phenotyping, AI in psychiatry) be governed to prevent new forms of coercion or rights infringements?

macarthur-perceived-coercion-scale.title: MacArthur Perceived Coercion Scale
macarthur-perceived-coercion-scale.plainText: MacArthur Perceived Coercion Scale Why It Matters Measures subjective experience of coercion in psychiatric treatment contexts Distinguishes between objective pressure and perceived voluntariness in clinical and research settings Informs ethical assessment of consent validity under conditions of influence Supports evaluation of coercive practices beyond mere presence of external pressure Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Perceived coercion depends on specific qualities of influence | Voluntariness in research consent requires influences to be external, intentional, illegitimate, and causally effective—not merely the presence of pressure or incentives | Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 22 | | Measurement of perceived coercion is essential for ethical evaluation | The MacArthur Perceived Coercion Scale captures the subjective dimension of coercion that objective measures miss | Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 22 | Sub-topics perceived-coercion Related concepts voluntariness informed-consent autonomy coercion coercion-in-psychiatry research-ethics ethical-decision-making patient-autonomy decision-making-capacity capacity-assessment Open questions How does the MacArthur Perceived Coercion Scale compare to other measures of subjective coercion across different cultural or legal contexts? What are the thresholds on the MacArthur Perceived Coercion Scale that indicate invalid consent in research or treatment settings? To what extent do clinical staff recognize discrepancies between objective coercion and patient-reported perceived coercion? How should findings from the MacArthur Perceived Coercion Scale inform policy reforms regarding coercive practices in psychiatry?

neuroethics.title: neuroethics
neuroethics.plainText: neuroethics Why It Matters Explores ethical limits of chemical restraints in psychiatric care Challenges traditional views on bodily autonomy and consent Highlights gaps in principlist approaches to clinical ethics Informs policy debates on coercive treatment and human rights Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |----------|------------|-------------| | Chemical restraints altering consciousness are greater liberty violations | Changing desires or conscious states constitutes a deeper infringement on basic liberty than unconsciousness | 2024 - Birks - Chemical Restraints and the Basic Liberties | | Sedating chemical restraints are more ethically problematic | Restraints that undermine consciousness are worse than those altering specific states due to necessary basic liberty infringement | 2024 - Crutchfield, Redinger - Basic Liberties, Consent, and Chemical Restraints | | Principlism is inadequate for chemical restraint ethics | Overemphasizes autonomy, lacks value theory for conflict resolution, ignores collective moral impact | 2024 - Mangal, Scheiner - The Limitations of Principlism | Sub-topics chemical-restraint basic-liberty autonomy informed-consent beneficence non-maleficence coercion least-restrictive-alternative human-rights biomedical-model Related concepts clinical-ethics psychiatric-ethics biomedical-model biopsychosocial-model human-rights-based-approach disability-rights convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with-disabilities informed-consent capacity-assessment substitute-decision-making Open questions How should basic liberty be balanced against therapeutic necessity in emergency psychiatry? Can value-sensitive frameworks resolve conflicts where principlism fails in restraint decisions? What level of consciousness alteration justifies invoking stronger liberty protections?
neuroethics.richText:
##Why It Matters

Explores ethical limits of chemical restraints in psychiatric care Challenges traditional views on bodily autonomy and consent Highlights gaps in principlist approaches to clinical ethics Informs policy debates on coercive treatment and human rights

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |----------|------------|-------------| | Chemical restraints altering consciousness are greater liberty violations | Changing desires or conscious states constitutes a deeper infringement on basic liberty than unconsciousness | {{2024 - Birks - Chemical Restraints and the Basic Liberties}} | | Sedating chemical restraints are more ethically problematic | Restraints that undermine consciousness are worse than those altering specific states due to necessary basic liberty infringement | {{2024 - Crutchfield, Redinger - Basic Liberties, Consent, and Chemical Restraints}} | | Principlism is inadequate for chemical restraint ethics | Overemphasizes autonomy, lacks value theory for conflict resolution, ignores collective moral impact | {{2024 - Mangal, Scheiner - The Limitations of Principlism}} |

##Sub-topics

chemical-restraint basic-liberty autonomy informed-consent beneficence non-maleficence coercion least-restrictive-alternative human-rights biomedical-model

##Related concepts

clinical-ethics psychiatric-ethics biomedical-model biopsychosocial-model human-rights-based-approach disability-rights convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with-disabilities informed-consent capacity-assessment substitute-decision-making

##Open questions

How should basic liberty be balanced against therapeutic necessity in emergency psychiatry? Can value-sensitive frameworks resolve conflicts where principlism fails in restraint decisions? What level of consciousness alteration justifies invoking stronger liberty protections?

psychiatric-ethics.title: psychiatric ethics
psychiatric-ethics.plainText: psychiatric ethics Why It Matters Ethical frameworks guide clinical decisions in psychiatric care where coercion, consent, and capacity intersect. Cultural and contextual variation challenges universal application of ethical principles in global psychiatry. Emerging issues like chemical restraints require re-evaluation of autonomy and liberty in treatment settings. International efforts aim to harmonize ethical standards through shared guidelines and lived experience integration. Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Ethical principles are context-dependent | Principles vary across cultural, religious, economic, and political settings; not universally applicable | 2020 - Atakora, Asampong - Ethical Principles in Psychiatric Practice; A Systematic Review | | WPA Code provides global guidance | First comprehensive WPA Code (2020) adapts medical ethics principles for clinical, educational, research, and advocacy use worldwide | 2021 - Appelbaum, Tyano - The WPA Code of Ethics for Psychiatry | | Coercion requires justification | Permissible only in exceptional cases when serving higher values (e.g., long-term autonomy, safety) after transparent evaluation | 2021 - Chieze et al. - Coercive Measures in Psychiatry A Review of Ethical Arguments | | Structured decision-making for coercion | Ethical decisions should follow algorithms based on capacity, benefit-harm analysis, and biopsychosocial context | 2023 - Steinert - Using coercion in mental disorders or risking the patients death An analysis of the protocols of a clinical ethics committee | | Chemical restraints threaten liberty | Sedating agents that undermine consciousness infringe basic liberties more than those altering specific states | 2024 - Crutchfield, Redinger - Basic Liberties, Consent, and Chemical Restraints | | Lived experience must center ethics | Patient experience reveals how drugs undermine authentic consent and perpetuate epistemic injustice and sanism | 2024 - Dougherty et al. - The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint; Prioritizing Patient Experience | | Advance directives reduce coercion | Enable prior consent and align crisis care with pre-expressed preferences, reducing reliance on coercion | 2012 - Bonnie - Advance Directives A Tool for Reducing Coercion | | Europe favors shared ethical frameworks | National associations rely on international codes; support EPA-led collaboration for shared guideline databases | 2023 - Samochowiec et al. - Ethical dilemmas in contemporary psychiatry; Findings from a survey of National Psychiatric Associations in Europe | | Traditional vs. Western views on coercion | In traditional societies, coercion may be socially acceptable when aligned with family/religious values; contrasts with Western autonomy focus | Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - psychiatric-ethics.richText:
##Why It Matters

Ethical frameworks guide clinical decisions in psychiatric care where coercion, consent, and capacity intersect. Cultural and contextual variation challenges universal application of ethical principles in global psychiatry. Emerging issues like chemical restraints require re-evaluation of autonomy and liberty in treatment settings. International efforts aim to harmonize ethical standards through shared guidelines and lived experience integration.

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Ethical principles are context-dependent | Principles vary across cultural, religious, economic, and political settings; not universally applicable | {{2020 - Atakora, Asampong - Ethical Principles in Psychiatric Practice; A Systematic Review}} | | WPA Code provides global guidance | First comprehensive WPA Code (2020) adapts medical ethics principles for clinical, educational, research, and advocacy use worldwide | {{2021 - Appelbaum, Tyano - The WPA Code of Ethics for Psychiatry}} | | Coercion requires justification | Permissible only in exceptional cases when serving higher values (e.g., long-term autonomy, safety) after transparent evaluation | {{2021 - Chieze et al. - Coercive Measures in Psychiatry A Review of Ethical Arguments}} | | Structured decision-making for coercion | Ethical decisions should follow algorithms based on capacity, benefit-harm analysis, and biopsychosocial context | {{2023 - Steinert - Using coercion in mental disorders or risking the patients death An analysis of the protocols of a clinical ethics committee}} | | Chemical restraints threaten liberty | Sedating agents that undermine consciousness infringe basic liberties more than those altering specific states | {{2024 - Crutchfield, Redinger - Basic Liberties, Consent, and Chemical Restraints}} | | Lived experience must center ethics | Patient experience reveals how drugs undermine authentic consent and perpetuate epistemic injustice and sanism | {{2024 - Dougherty et al. - The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint; Prioritizing Patient Experience}} | | Advance directives reduce coercion | Enable prior consent and align crisis care with pre-expressed preferences, reducing reliance on coercion | {{2012 - Bonnie - Advance Directives A Tool for Reducing Coercion}} | | Europe favors shared ethical frameworks | National associations rely on international codes; support EPA-led collaboration for shared guideline databases | {{2023 - Samochowiec et al. - Ethical dilemmas in contemporary psychiatry; Findings from a survey of National Psychiatric Associations in Europe}} | | Traditional vs. Western views on coercion | In traditional societies, coercion may be socially acceptable when aligned with family/religious values; contrasts with Western autonomy focus | {{Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 13}} |
