involuntary-commitment.title: involuntary commitment
involuntary-commitment.plainText: involuntary commitment Why It Matters Involuntary commitment balances individual liberty with public safety through legal and clinical standards Central to ethical debates on autonomy, coercion, and human rights in mental health care Informs legal reforms and clinical practices regarding psychiatric hospitalization and treatment Intersects with disability rights, due process, and international human rights frameworks Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|-----------|-------------| | Constitutional right to refuse treatment | Patients retain a qualified right to refuse antipsychotics, overridable only upon incompetence or dangerousness | 1980 - Brooks - The constitutional right to refuse antipsychotic medications. | | Limits on coercion feasibility | Only 31% of European mental health professionals believe total abolition of psychiatric coercion is achievable due to safety and untreated illness concerns | 2024 - Birkeland et al. - Abolition of coercion in mental health services - A European survey of feasibility | | Legal justification for emergency commitment | Involuntary hospitalization justified by police power (danger to self/others), not parens patriae, requiring procedural compliance and least restrictive means | Clinical Handbook of Psychiatry and Law - 02 | | International human rights standards | Involuntary treatment must meet proportionality, necessity, and procedural safeguards under international law to protect liberty and privacy | The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 03 | Sub-topics civil-commitment involuntary-admission involuntary-hospitalization involuntary-treatment involuntary-medication forced-medication compulsory-treatment emergency-psychiatry psychiatric-emergency court-ordered-treatment Related concepts autonomy bodily-autonomy decisional-capacity informed-consent least-restrictive-principle proportionality due-process human-rights coercion coercion-in-psychiatry coercive-measures patient-safety risk-assessment therapeutic-jurisprudence disability-rights convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with-disabilities crpd Open questions Under what conditions can involuntary commitment be justified without violating fundamental rights to liberty and bodily integrity? How can mental health systems reduce reliance on coercion while ensuring safety for individuals and others? What role should advance directives and supported decision-making play in limiting involuntary interventions? How do cultural and legal differences affect the application and perception of involuntary commitment across jurisdictions?
involuntary-commitment.richText:
##Why It Matters

Involuntary commitment balances individual liberty with public safety through legal and clinical standards Central to ethical debates on autonomy, coercion, and human rights in mental health care Informs legal reforms and clinical practices regarding psychiatric hospitalization and treatment Intersects with disability rights, due process, and international human rights frameworks

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|-----------|-------------| | Constitutional right to refuse treatment | Patients retain a qualified right to refuse antipsychotics, overridable only upon incompetence or dangerousness | {{1980 - Brooks - The constitutional right to refuse antipsychotic medications.}} | | Limits on coercion feasibility | Only 31% of European mental health professionals believe total abolition of psychiatric coercion is achievable due to safety and untreated illness concerns | {{2024 - Birkeland et al. - Abolition of coercion in mental health services - A European survey of feasibility}} | | Legal justification for emergency commitment | Involuntary hospitalization justified by police power (danger to self/others), not parens patriae, requiring procedural compliance and least restrictive means | {{Clinical Handbook of Psychiatry and Law - 02}} | | International human rights standards | Involuntary treatment must meet proportionality, necessity, and procedural safeguards under international law to protect liberty and privacy | {{The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 03}} |

mental-illness-stigma.title: mental illness stigma
mental-illness-stigma.plainText: mental illness stigma Why It Matters Central to understanding public attitudes that drive approval of coercive psychiatric measures Interacts with ethical tensions in mental health law and practice Influences policy debates around involuntary treatment and human rights protections Shapes patient experiences of care, autonomy, and dignity in psychiatric settings Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |--------|-----------|------------| | Stigma drives coercion approval | Public support for psychiatric coercion increases with perceived dangerousness and is amplified by stigmatizing attitudes, especially under severe measures | 2022 - Steiger et al. - Approval of Coercion in Psychiatry in Public Perception and the Role of Stigmatization | | Ethical split in psychiatry | Psychiatry faces unique ethical conflict due to utilitarian mental health legislation coexisting with rights-based laws, unlike other medical specialties | 2008 - Lepping - Is psychiatry torn in different ethical directions | Sub-topics No specific sub-topic concepts identified in the existing concept list that directly refine "mental illness stigma" as a navigational hub. Related concepts coercion, coercion-in-psychiatry, human-rights, psychiatric-ethics, stigma, ethical-conflict, patient-autonomy, involuntary-treatment, civil-commitment, therapeutic-relationship Open questions How does stigma differentially affect attitudes toward various forms of coercion (e.g., medication vs. seclusion)? Can anti-stigma interventions reduce public acceptance of coercive practices in psychiatry? What role does stigma play in the ethical tension between utilitarian mental health law and rights-based frameworks? Are certain diagnostic groups disproportionately impacted by stigma-driven coercion endorsement?

##Why It Matters

Central to understanding public attitudes that drive approval of coercive psychiatric measures Interacts with ethical tensions in mental health law and practice Influences policy debates around involuntary treatment and human rights protections Shapes patient experiences of care, autonomy, and dignity in psychiatric settings

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |--------|-----------|------------| | Stigma drives coercion approval | Public support for psychiatric coercion increases with perceived dangerousness and is amplified by stigmatizing attitudes, especially under severe measures | {{2022 - Steiger et al. - Approval of Coercion in Psychiatry in Public Perception and the Role of Stigmatization}} | | Ethical split in psychiatry | Psychiatry faces unique ethical conflict due to utilitarian mental health legislation coexisting with rights-based laws, unlike other medical specialties | {{2008 - Lepping - Is psychiatry torn in different ethical directions}} |

least-restrictive-treatment.title: least restrictive treatment
least-restrictive-treatment.plainText: least restrictive treatment Why It Matters Ensures patient liberty is prioritized in psychiatric treatment settings Provides a legal and ethical framework for minimizing coercion in mental healthcare Supports the development of alternatives to involuntary interventions Balances treatment efficacy with human rights protections in clinical practice Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Legal standard for civil commitment | Requires "clear and convincing" evidence of mental illness and dangerousness or grave disability | Psychiatry and the Law - 06 | | Framework for reducing coercion | Integrates human rights principles with contextual factors like family involvement and legal reform to define and reduce coercive measures | 2013 - Lepping, Raveesh - The Mysore Declaration | Sub-topics least-restrictive-alternative least-restrictive-intervention least-restrictive-principle Related concepts autonomy basic-liberty beneficence coercion coercive-measures civil-commitment decisions-making-capacity informed-consent involuntary-treatment patient-autonomy therapeutic-misconception voluntariness Open questions How can the least restrictive treatment standard be consistently applied across different cultural and legal contexts? What metrics best determine when a less restrictive alternative has been adequately attempted before resorting to coercion? To what extent does family involvement, as emphasized in the Mysore Declaration, influence outcomes under least restrictive frameworks? How do resource limitations in public mental health systems affect the feasibility of implementing least restrictive treatment?

therapeutic-privilege.title: therapeutic privilege
therapeutic-privilege.plainText: therapeutic privilege Why It Matters Addresses ethical tensions in psychiatric decision-making when disclosure may harm the patient Challenges assumptions about universal applicability of informed consent standards Highlights cultural and contextual variability in clinical ethics Intersects with legal standards on capacity and substituted consent Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Contextual ethics | Ethical principles in psychiatry depend on cultural, religious, economic, and political settings | 2020 - Atakora, Asampong - Ethical Principles in Psychiatric Practice; A Systematic Review | | Functional competency | Competence for consent is assessed functionally, not by diagnosis, and refusal rights are qualified by law | Clinical Manual of Psychiatry and Law - 04 | Sub-topics informed-consent competency-assessment substituted-decision-making Related concepts autonomy beneficence non-maleficence paternalism patient-autonomy therapeutic-relationship Open questions How should clinicians balance therapeutic privilege with patient autonomy across diverse cultural contexts? What safeguards prevent misuse of therapeutic privilege to justify paternalistic overreach? Are there evidence-based guidelines for determining when nondisclosure is ethically justified? How do legal standards for substituted consent interface with claims of therapeutic privilege?

risk-assessment.title: risk assessment
risk-assessment.plainText: risk assessment Why It Matters Enables prediction and prevention of imminent aggression in clinical settings Balances safety imperatives with ethical and legal obligations in patient care Informs decisions about coercive measures through structured, evidence-based tools Supports minimization of restraint by identifying alternatives like de-escalation Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |----------|------------|-------------| | Structured tools predict imminent aggression | Brøset Violence Checklist and DASA show large effect sizes for 24-hour aggression prediction | 2020 - Dickens et al. - Structured assessments for imminent aggression in mental health and correctional settings Systematic review and meta-analysis | | Machine learning enhances prediction | Gradient boosting models show moderate to strong performance for violent behavior in schizophrenia, but lack external validation | 2024 - Parsaei et al. - Machine Learning for prediction of violent behaviors in schizophrenia spectrum disorders a systematic review | | Remote monitoring has potential but barriers | Staff see passive remote monitoring useful for early detection but cite safety, privacy, workload, and engagement challenges | 2019 - Greer et al. - Predicting Inpatient Aggression in Forensic Services Using Remote Monitoring Technology Qualitative Study of Staff Perspectives | | Staff knowledge gaps persist | Nurses have moderate overall knowledge of restraint but significant deficits in legal/ethical understanding despite safety beliefs | 2016 - Cunha et al. - Chemical and Physical Restraint of Patients | | Restraint poses significant risks | Physical and psychological harm, autonomy violations necessitate minimization via safeguards and de-escalation | 2024 - Paul et al. - Exploring psychiatric patient restraints Balancing safety, ethics, and patient rights in mental healthcare | | Coercion should be minimized | De-escalation techniques prevent aggression by addressing underlying needs before violence escalates | Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 07 | | Coercion as last resort | In forensic psychiatry, coercive measures remain necessary in extreme situations despite preference for prevention and training | The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 21 | Sub-topics br-set-violence-checklist de-escalation de-escalation-techniques dynamic-appraisal-of-situational-aggression machine-learning risk-assessment (see violence-risk-assessment) violence-risk-assessment Related concepts coercion coercive-measures coercive-treatment chemical-restraint physical-restraint restraint seclusion patient-autonomy informed-consent least-restrictive-principle last-resort-principle beneficence non-maleficence autonomy human-rights therapeutic-relationship trauma-informed-care recovery-oriented-care shared-decision-making involuntary-treatment forensic-psychiatry mental-health-law ethical-decision-making clinical-ethics Open questions How can machine learning models for violence prediction be validated across diverse, real-world clinical populations? Under what conditions does remote monitoring improve early aggression detection without compromising privacy or therapeutic alliance? How do structured risk assessment tools perform in predicting aggression beyond 24 hours in long-term care settings?

chemical-restraints.title: chemical restraints
chemical-restraints.plainText: chemical restraints Why It Matters Central to ethical and legal debates in emergency and psychiatric care regarding patient autonomy and safety Highlights tensions between clinical necessity and fundamental rights, especially when consciousness is altered Underscores limitations of dominant ethical frameworks like principlism in addressing collective and relational impacts Drives policy and practice around de-escalation, informed consent, and regulatory compliance Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | De-escalation-first approach | Physical and chemical restraints should be used only after de-escalation fails, with strict protocols | 2020 - Guerrero et al. - Physical and Chemical Restraints (an Update) | | Ethical concern with consciousness-altering restraints | Chemical restraints that sedate or undermine consciousness are more ethically problematic due to infringement on basic liberties | 2024 - Crutchfield, Redinger - Basic Liberties, Consent, and Chemical Restraints | | Critique of principlism | Principlism is inadequate for ethical decisions on chemical restraints as it overemphasizes autonomy and ignores communal moral impact | 2024 - Mangal, Scheiner - The Limitations of Principlism | Sub-topics de-escalation informed-consent basic-liberty autonomy physical-restraint antipsychotics benzodiazepines rapid-tranquilization Related concepts beneficence non-maleficence least-restrictive-alternative involuntary-medication coercion patient-safety therapeutic-alliance human-rights Open questions How can ethical frameworks better balance individual autonomy with communal moral considerations in the use of chemical restraints? What are the long-term psychological and neurological impacts of consciousness-altering chemical restraints, and how should they inform consent processes? To what extent do current regulatory and training protocols effectively prevent misuse while allowing clinically necessary use? How might supported or shared decision-making models be adapted for use in emergency contexts involving chemical restraints?

police-power.title: police power
police-power.plainText: police power Why It Matters Central to legal justification for involuntary psychiatric interventions Defines limits of state authority over individual bodily autonomy in mental health contexts Balances public safety concerns with protection of civil liberties Underpins frameworks for civil commitment and forced treatment Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Limited state power to override treatment refusal | Involuntary medication requires finding of incompetence or dangerousness; privacy rights create qualified right to refuse | 1980 - Brooks - The constitutional right to refuse antipsychotic medications. | | High burden for civil commitment | Commitment demands "clear and convincing" evidence of mental illness and dangerousness or grave disability | Psychiatry and the Law - 06 | Sub-topics civil-commitment involuntary-treatment forced-medication informed-consent autonomy Related concepts competence-assessment least-restrictive-alternative patient-autonomy civil-liberties medical-model Open questions How should "dangerousness" be operationalized to prevent misuse of police power? What procedural safeguards are necessary to protect against erroneous deprivation of liberty? To what extent does police power in psychiatry perpetuate systemic biases against marginalized groups? Can advance directives meaningfully constrain state police power during psychiatric crises?

##Open questions

How should "dangerousness" be operationalized to prevent misuse of police power? What procedural safeguards are necessary to protect against erroneous deprivation of liberty? To what extent does police power in psychiatry perpetuate systemic biases against marginalized groups? Can advance directives meaningfully constrain state police power during psychiatric crises?

mental-capacity-act-2005.title: Mental Capacity Act 2005
mental-capacity-act-2005.plainText: Mental Capacity Act 2005 What It Is The Mental Capacity Act 2005 is a UK law that provides a legal framework for decision-making on behalf of individuals who lack the mental capacity to make specific decisions for themselves. It establishes principles for assessing capacity, making best-interest decisions, and safeguarding the rights of vulnerable adults in health and social care contexts. It is central to ethical and legal discussions about autonomy and paternalism in clinical practice. Role in the Literature In 2008 - Lepping - Is psychiatry torn in different ethical directions: Legislation supporting decision-making for those who may lack capacity, central to the rights-focused framework and capacity assessment requirements. In 2016 - Lepping et al. - Paternalismivi. autonomy - are we barking up the wrong tree: UK law permitting coercion when patients lack capacity, central to the discussion of autonomy vs. paternalism in England and Wales
mental-capacity-act-2005.richText:
##What It Is

The Mental Capacity Act 2005 is a UK law that provides a legal framework for decision-making on behalf of individuals who lack the mental capacity to make specific decisions for themselves. It establishes principles for assessing capacity, making best-interest decisions, and safeguarding the rights of vulnerable adults in health and social care contexts. It is central to ethical and legal discussions about autonomy and paternalism in clinical practice.

##Role in the Literature

In {{2008 - Lepping - Is psychiatry torn in different ethical directions}}: Legislation supporting decision-making for those who may lack capacity, central to the rights-focused framework and capacity assessment requirements. In {{2016 - Lepping et al. - Paternalismivi. autonomy - are we barking up the wrong tree}}: UK law permitting coercion when patients lack capacity, central to the discussion of autonomy vs. paternalism in England and Wales

treatment-gap.title: treatment gap
treatment-gap.plainText: treatment gap Why It Matters The concept of treatment gap highlights systemic failures in mental health care delivery, particularly the lack of access to effective treatment for severe conditions. It underscores how critiques of psychiatry that ignore treatment gaps may misattribute human rights violations to biomedical approaches rather than systemic under-resourcing. Addressing treatment gaps is essential for evidence-based care and reducing preventable suffering in mental health systems. Recognizing treatment gaps supports arguments for increasing investment in mental health services as a human rights imperative. Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Treatment gaps as systemic issue | Human rights critiques of psychiatry often overlook how lack of access to effective treatment contributes to crises, unfairly blaming biomedical models. | 2018 - Dharmawardene et al. - Responding to the UN Special Rapporteur's anti-psychiatry bias | | Rights-based shift needed | Mental health systems must move from coercive models to rights-based approaches under CRPD, prioritizing autonomy and rejecting involuntary interventions. | 2019 - Puras et al. - Mental health and human rights in the 21st century | Sub-topics No matching sub-topics found in the existing concept list for "treatment gap". Related concepts human-rights convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with-disabilities crpd-article-12 coercion coercion-in-psychiatry involuntary-treatment autonomy informed-consent biomedical-model evidence-based-medicine Open questions How can mental health systems balance the need to reduce treatment gaps with the imperative to eliminate coercive practices? What evidence exists on the effectiveness of scaling up voluntary, community-based mental health services in closing treatment gaps? To what extent do current human rights frameworks adequately address systemic underfunding as a structural violence in mental health? How should resource allocation decisions be made when expanding access risks reintroducing forms of institutional control?

recovery-oriented-care.title: recovery-oriented care
recovery-oriented-care.plainText: recovery-oriented care Why It Matters Integrates patient autonomy and lived experience into mental health treatment planning Reduces reliance on coercive practices by prioritizing recovery goals and self-determination Aligns with human rights frameworks and ethical standards in psychiatric care Supports implementation of advance directives and alternatives to coercion as tools for empowerment Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |--------|------------|-------------| | Patient-centered recovery focus | Recovery-oriented care emphasizes personal goals, hope, and agency over symptom control alone | 2012 - Bonnie - Advance Directives A Tool for Reducing Coercion, 2020 - Rodrigues - Implementing Alternatives to Coercion; A Key Component of Improving Mental Health Care | | Reduction of coercion as core aim | Implementing recovery-oriented approaches directly reduces use of coercive measures by aligning care with prior expressed wishes | 2012 - Bonnie - Advance Directives A Tool for Reducing Coercion, 2020 - Rodrigues - Implementing Alternatives to Coercion; A Key Component of Improving Mental Health Care | | Importance of patient experience | Service users’ experiences show that safety, dignity, and participation in care reduce perceptions of coercion, even in community-based models | 2023 - Brekke et al. - Patients' experiences with coercive mental health treatment in Flexible Assertive Community Treatment a qualitative study | | Systemic distrust persists | Despite improvements in care quality, forced medication and systemic issues continue to undermine trust in mental health services | 2023 - Brekke et al. - Patients' experiences with coercive mental health treatment in Flexible Assertive Community Treatment a qualitative study | Sub-topics advance-directives psychiatric-advance-directives person-centered-care supported-decision-making shared-decision-making trauma-informed-care least-restrictive-principle human-rights-based-approach Related concepts autonomy coercion coercion-in-psychiatry involuntary-treatment patient-autonomy patient-experience human-dignity ethical-decision-making de-escalation informed-consent Open questions How can recovery-oriented principles be consistently applied across diverse mental health service models, including involuntary care settings? How do systemic factors like institutional distrust and power imbalances limit the real-world impact of recovery-oriented approaches? In what ways might recovery-oriented care conflict with clinical risk assessments, and how should such tensions be resolved ethically?

rapid-tranquilization.title: rapid tranquilization
rapid-tranquilization.plainText: rapid tranquilization Why It Matters Central term in debates about chemical restraint and psychiatric emergency interventions Reflects clinical practice patterns for managing acute agitation Highlights tensions between efficacy, safety, and ethical use of pharmacological interventions Frequently conflated with related terms, complicating research and policy Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Clinical preference for antipsychotics in acute agitation | Haloperidol alone or with anticholinergics is preferred due to stronger evidence and better risk profile than benzodiazepines alone | 2019 - Zareifopoulos et al. - Treatment Options for Acute Agitation in Psychiatric Patients Theoretical and Empirical Evidence | | Definitional ambiguity hinders practice and research | "Rapid tranquilization" is used interchangeably with "chemical restraint" and "therapeutic sedation," creating confusion in clinical, legal, and ethical contexts | 2020 - Muir-Cochrane - A wicked problem Chemical restraint towards a definition | | Risks necessitate minimization and oversight | Psychiatric restraints (including pharmacological) pose significant harms and should be reduced via legal safeguards, de-escalation, and monitoring | 2024 - Paul et al. - Exploring psychiatric patient restraints Balancing safety, ethics, and patient rights in mental healthcare | Sub-topics chemical-restraint de-escalation least-restrictive-alternative patient-safety Related concepts antipsychotics benzodiazepines coercion informed-consent involuntary-treatment patient-autonomy psychiatric-emergency restraint therapeutic-sedation Open questions How can rapid tranquilization be clearly distinguished from chemical restraint and therapeutic sedation in clinical guidelines? What evidence supports the safety and efficacy of specific regimens (e.g., haloperidol-promethazine) across diverse populations? To what extent does the use of rapid tranquilization violate principles of autonomy and informed consent in emergency contexts? How do legal and ethical frameworks govern the use of rapid tranquilization across different jurisdictions?

involuntary-treatment.title: involuntary treatment
involuntary-treatment.plainText: involuntary treatment Why It Matters Central to debates about patient autonomy and clinical safety in psychiatric care Intersects with legal, ethical, and human rights frameworks across jurisdictions Reflects evolving societal values regarding coercion in mental health treatment Highlights tensions between beneficence, non-maleficence, and respect for persons Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|-----------|-------------| | Ethical decision-making is socially driven | Psychiatric ethical judgments arise from team emotions and social dynamics, not pure reason; joint-commission.title: Joint Commission
joint-commission.plainText: Joint Commission What It Is The Joint Commission is a nonprofit organization that accredits and certifies healthcare organizations and programs in the United States. It establishes evidence-based standards for patient safety and quality of care, playing a critical role in shaping clinical practices across healthcare settings. Role in the Literature In 2016 - Cunha et al. - Chemical and Physical Restraint of Patients: Referenced for its evidence-based guidelines on restraint use, including time-limited orders and mandatory reevaluation protocols In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 12: Referenced as an organization that audits procedural safety and security at a macro-level in healthcare institutions.
joint-commission.richText:
##What It Is

The Joint Commission is a nonprofit organization that accredits and certifies healthcare organizations and programs in the United States. It establishes evidence-based standards for patient safety and quality of care, playing a critical role in shaping clinical practices across healthcare settings.

##Role in the Literature

In {{2016 - Cunha et al. - Chemical and Physical Restraint of Patients}}: Referenced for its evidence-based guidelines on restraint use, including time-limited orders and mandatory reevaluation protocols In {{Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 12}}: Referenced as an organization that audits procedural safety and security at a macro-level in healthcare institutions.

procedural-justice.title: procedural justice
procedural-justice.plainText: procedural justice Why It Matters Central to evaluating the legitimacy of coercive practices in mental healthcare Addresses fairness in decision-making processes, independent of outcomes Highlights stakeholder conflicts over what constitutes just procedures Serves as a ethical and legal safeguard against arbitrary use of power Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Procedural justice as a condition for justifying coercion | Coercion may be permissible only if fair procedures (e.g., necessity, proportionality, voice) are followed, even under harm principle or soft paternalism | Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 08 | | Stakeholder divergence on procedural fairness | Service users view coercion as rights violation; staff and caregivers see it as necessary despite acknowledging harm to dignity and relationships | Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 04 | | Limits of procedural justice | Some argue coercion can never be justified under human rights frameworks, regardless of procedural safeguards | Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 08 | Sub-topics Due-process Related concepts Coercion, Coercion-in-psychiatry, Coercive-measures, Coercive-measures-in-psychiatry, Coercive-treatment, Human-rights, Legal-capacity, Least-restrictive-alternative, Least-restrictive-principle, Patient-autonomy, Substitute-decision-making, Substituted-decision-making, Substituted-judgment, Supported-decision-making Open questions How can procedural justice be meaningfully measured or assessed in high-pressure psychiatric emergency contexts? What weight should be given to service user experiences of procedural fairness versus institutional assessments of necessity? Are there viable alternatives to coercion that satisfy both safety concerns and procedural justice ideals? To what extent do current legal and institutional procedures actually uphold procedural justice, or merely ritualize compliance?

Ye et al. - Staff Training Reduces the Use of Physical Restraint in Mental Health Service, Evidence-based Reflection for China | | Critique of international oversight | UN Special Rapporteur's report displays anti-psychiatry bias by overlooking treatment efficacy and systemic underfunding | 2018 - Dharmawardene et al. - Responding to the UN Special Rapporteur's anti-psychiatry bias | | Human rights-centered approach | Mental health policy must prevent abuses while ensuring access to effective care, especially for severe cases | 2018 - Dharmawardene et al. - Responding to the UN Special Rapporteur's anti-psychiatry bias | | Safety through training | Investing in staff competency decreases use of coercive measures like physical restraint | 2018 - Ye et al. - Staff Training Reduces the Use of Physical Restraint in Mental Health Service, Evidence-based Reflection for China | Sub-topics physical-restraint coercive-measures-in-psychiatry involuntary-treatment patient-safety human-rights evidence-based-practice staff-training Related concepts coercion-in-psychiatry least-restrictive-alternative informed-consent patient-autonomy biomedical-model recovery-oriented-care de-escalation involuntary-hospitalization mental-health-law human-rights-based-approach Open questions How can mental health policies effectively reduce coercive practices without compromising patient or staff safety? mental-health-policy.richText:
##Why It Matters

Addresses systemic gaps in mental health care while balancing evidence-based treatment and human rights protections Examines how policy frameworks influence coercive practices such as restraint and involuntary treatment Informs ethical and legal standards for patient autonomy and safety in psychiatric settings Highlights tensions between biomedical approaches and critiques of psychiatric power structures

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Evidence-based reform | Staff training reduces reliance on physical restraint, improving safety and care quality | {{2018 - Ye et al. - Staff Training Reduces the Use of Physical Restraint in Mental Health Service, Evidence-based Reflection for China}} | | Critique of international oversight | UN Special Rapporteur's report displays anti-psychiatry bias by overlooking treatment efficacy and systemic underfunding | {{2018 - Dharmawardene et al. - Responding to the UN Special Rapporteur's anti-psychiatry bias}} | | Human rights-centered approach | Mental health policy must prevent abuses while ensuring access to effective care, especially for severe cases | {{2018 - Dharmawardene et al. - Responding to the UN Special Rapporteur's anti-psychiatry bias}} | | Safety through training | Investing in staff competency decreases use of coercive measures like physical restraint | {{2018 - Ye et al. - Staff Training Reduces the Use of Physical Restraint in Mental Health Service, Evidence-based Reflection for China}} |

##Sub-topics

physical-restraint coercive-measures-in-psychiatry involuntary-treatment patient-safety human-rights evidence-based-practice staff-training

##Related concepts

coercion-in-psychiatry least-restrictive-alternative informed-consent patient-autonomy biomedical-model recovery-oriented-care de-escalation involuntary-hospitalization mental-health-law human-rights-based-approach

##Open questions

How can mental health policies effectively reduce coercive practices without compromising patient or staff safety? What balance should be struck between respecting international human rights critiques and defending the legitimacy of biomedical psychiatry in treatment-resistant cases? To what extent should staff training be mandated and funded as a core component of mental health policy reform? How do cultural and systemic factors influence the implementation of evidence-based policies across different national contexts?

least-restrictive-alternative.title: least restrictive alternative
least-restrictive-alternative.plainText: least restrictive alternative Why It Matters Guides ethical and legal use of restraint and coercion in psychiatric settings Balances individual autonomy with clinical and safety imperatives Serves as a legal and procedural safeguard in involuntary treatment decisions Reflects evolving standards from social control to rights-based frameworks Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Individualized approach | Service users' preferences should determine the least restrictive restraint type | 2024 - Hempeler et al. - Preferences of Individual Mental Health Service Users Are Essential in Determining the Least Restrictive Type of Restraint | | Legal standard | Involuntary hospitalization must comply with least restrictive principles and procedural standards | Clinical Handbook of Psychiatry and Law - 02 | | Rights-based challenge | Persons with psychosocial disabilities retain legal capacity and must consent to interventions | Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 09 | | Ethical tension | Coercion remains ethically contested due to conflicts between autonomy and beneficence | Human Rights in Psychiatry - 06 | | Practical limitation | No universal best practice standards for coercive treatment exist due to methodological limits | Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 06 | | Limited impact | Right to treatment ensures only minimally adequate care, not optimal or chosen treatment | Clinical Handbook of Psychiatry and Law - 03 | Sub-topics least-restrictive-intervention least-restrictive-means least-restrictive-principle least-restrictive-treatment Related concepts autonomy beneficence coercion-in-psychiatry convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with-disabilities crpd crpd-article-12 involuntary-treatment legal-capacity parens-patriae patient-centered-care shared-decision-making supported-decision-making Open questions How can individual preferences be systematically integrated into least restrictive decision-making during acute crisis? What mechanisms ensure accountability when least restrictive principles are overridden in practice? How do cultural and systemic factors affect the application of least restrictive standards across jurisdictions? Is the least restrictive framework sufficient to prevent coercion, or does it require structural reforms beyond individual decision-making?
least-restrictive-alternative.richText:
##Why It Matters

Guides ethical and legal use of restraint and coercion in psychiatric settings Balances individual autonomy with clinical and safety imperatives Serves as a legal and procedural safeguard in involuntary treatment decisions Reflects evolving standards from social control to rights-based frameworks

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Individualized approach | Service users' preferences should determine the least restrictive restraint type | {{2024 - Hempeler et al. - Preferences of Individual Mental Health Service Users Are Essential in Determining the Least Restrictive Type of Restraint}} | | Legal standard | Involuntary hospitalization must comply with least restrictive principles and procedural standards | {{Clinical Handbook of Psychiatry and Law - 02}} | | Rights-based challenge | Persons with psychosocial disabilities retain legal capacity and must consent to interventions | {{Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 09}} | | Ethical tension | Coercion remains ethically contested due to conflicts between autonomy and beneficence | {{Human Rights in Psychiatry - 06}} | | Practical limitation | No universal best practice standards for coercive treatment exist due to methodological limits | {{Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 06}} | | Limited impact | Right to treatment ensures only minimally adequate care, not optimal or chosen treatment | {{Clinical Handbook of Psychiatry and Law - 03}} |

##Sub-topics

{{least-restrictive-intervention}} {{least-restrictive-means}} {{least-restrictive-principle}} {{least-restrictive-treatment}}

##Related concepts

{{autonomy}} {{beneficence}} {{coercion-in-psychiatry}} {{convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with-disabilities}} crpd {{crpd-article-12}} {{involuntary-treatment}} {{legal-capacity}} {{parens-patriae}} {{patient-centered-care}} {{shared-decision-making}} {{supported-decision-making}}

##Open questions

How can individual preferences be systematically integrated into least restrictive decision-making during acute crisis? What mechanisms ensure accountability when least restrictive principles are overridden in practice? How do cultural and systemic factors affect the application of least restrictive standards across jurisdictions? Is the least restrictive framework sufficient to prevent coercion, or does it require structural reforms beyond individual decision-making?

acute-agitation.title: acute agitation
acute-agitation.plainText: acute agitation Why It Matters Central to acute psychiatric care as a frequent presentation requiring rapid intervention Highlights tension between effective symptom control and risks of pharmacological interventions Reveals gaps in clinical guidance for managing treatment-resistant cases Drives development of standardized, safety-focused protocols for emergent medication use Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |----------|------------|-------------| | First-line preference | Haloperidol alone or with anticholinergics preferred due to strong evidence base | 2019 - Zareifopoulos et al. - Treatment Options for Acute Agitation in Psychiatric Patients Theoretical and Empirical Evidence | | Benzodiazepine limitations | Benzodiazepines alone are less effective and carry higher risks despite common use | 2019 - Zareifopoulos et al. - Treatment Options for Acute Agitation in Psychiatric Patients Theoretical and Empirical Evidence | | Guideline gap | Critical lack of guidance for persistent agitation after first-line failure | 2021 - Searles Quick et al. - Which Emergent Medication Should I Give Next Repeated Use of Emergent Medications to Treat Acute Agitation | | Sequential approach need | Risk-informed, stepwise algorithms needed to guide subsequent medication choices | 2021 - Searles Quick et al. - Which Emergent Medication Should I Give Next Repeated Use of Emergent Medications to Treat Acute Agitation | | Polypharmacy concern | Emphasis on minimizing polypharmacy and adverse events in sequential treatment | 2021 - Searles Quick et al. - Which Emergent Medication Should I Give Next Repeated Use of Emergent Medications to Treat Acute Agitation | Sub-topics antipsychotics benzodiazepines chemical-restraint de-escalation rapid-tranquilization Related concepts informed-consent patient-safety risk-assessment least-restrictive-alternative trauma-informed-care Open questions What is the optimal sequencing of medications after first-line treatment failure for acute agitation? How can clinical protocols balance speed of agitation control with minimization of adverse effects? What strategies reduce reliance on polypharmacy while maintaining efficacy in persistent agitation? How should risk factors (e.g., QTc prolongation, delirium) guide medication selection in sequential treatment?
acute-agitation.richText:
##Why It Matters

Central to acute psychiatric care as a frequent presentation requiring rapid intervention Highlights tension between effective symptom control and risks of pharmacological interventions Reveals gaps in clinical guidance for managing treatment-resistant cases Drives development of standardized, safety-focused protocols for emergent medication use

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |----------|------------|-------------| | First-line preference | Haloperidol alone or with anticholinergics preferred due to strong evidence base | {{2019 - Zareifopoulos et al. - Treatment Options for Acute Agitation in Psychiatric Patients Theoretical and Empirical Evidence}} | | Benzodiazepine limitations | Benzodiazepines alone are less effective and carry higher risks despite common use | {{2019 - Zareifopoulos et al. - Treatment Options for Acute Agitation in Psychiatric Patients Theoretical and Empirical Evidence}} | | Guideline gap | Critical lack of guidance for persistent agitation after first-line failure | {{2021 - Searles Quick et al. - Which Emergent Medication Should I Give Next Repeated Use of Emergent Medications to Treat Acute Agitation}} | | Sequential approach need | Risk-informed, stepwise algorithms needed to guide subsequent medication choices | {{2021 - Searles Quick et al. - Which Emergent Medication Should I Give Next Repeated Use of Emergent Medications to Treat Acute Agitation}} | | Polypharmacy concern | Emphasis on minimizing polypharmacy and adverse events in sequential treatment | {{2021 - Searles Quick et al. - Which Emergent Medication Should I Give Next Repeated Use of Emergent Medications to Treat Acute Agitation}} |

antipsychotics benzodiazepines chemical-restraint de-escalation rapid-tranquilization

##Related concepts

##Open questions

What is the optimal sequencing of medications after first-line treatment failure for acute agitation? How can clinical protocols balance speed of agitation control with minimization of adverse effects? What strategies reduce reliance on polypharmacy while maintaining efficacy in persistent agitation? How should risk factors (e.g., QTc prolongation, delirium) guide medication selection in sequential treatment?

psychiatric-coercion.title: psychiatric coercion
psychiatric-coercion.plainText: psychiatric coercion Why It Matters Central to ethical and human rights debates in mental health care Involves fundamental tensions between autonomy, safety, and treatment efficacy Linked to systemic issues like diagnostic uncertainty and power imbalances Directly impacts patient outcomes, trust in services, and legal compliance Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Human rights violation | Psychiatric coercion violates bodily autonomy and international human rights law | 2020 - Bergan - The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care: A Human The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care; A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention, 2024 - Bergan - The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care; A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention, Human Rights in Psychiatry - 03, Human Rights in Psychiatry - 08 | | Limited justification | Only impairment (not dangerousness or paternalism) justifies coercion in specific forms like restraint or forced medication | 2012 - Seo et al. - Coercion in psychiatric care Can paternalism justify coercion | | Systemic drivers | Coercion results from inadequate legal frameworks, unclear criteria, and ignored bioethics | 2020 - Rzhevskaya et al. - Psychiatric coercion and violence ethical, legal and preventive aspects | | Predictive factors | Female gender, psychotic diagnosis, and involuntary admission predict non-emergency forced medication | 2023 - Meroni et al. - Treatment without consent in adult psychiatry inpatient units a retrospective study on predictive factors | | Limited reform impact | Education improves attitudes but fails to change behavior due to systemic barriers | 2021 - Every‐Palmer et al. - Applying Human Rights and Reducing Coercion in Psychiatry following Service User-Led Education; A Qualitative Study. | | Skepticism about abolition | Most professionals doubt total abolition is feasible due to safety and untreated illness concerns | 2024 - Birkeland et al. - Abolition of coercion in mental health services - A European survey of feasibility | | Definitional inconsistency | Varying definitions of restrictive practices undermine research validity and comparisons | 2024 - Muluneh et al. - Variations in definitions used for describing restrictive care practices (seclusion and restraint) in adult mental health inpatient units a systematic | Sub-topics involuntary-treatment involuntary-medication forced-medication seclusion-and-restraint chemical-restraint mechanical-restraint physical-restraint involuntary-admission civil-commitment community-treatment-order Related concepts autonomy bodily-autonomy human-rights informed-consent last-resort-principle least-restrictive-alternative paternalism beneficence non-maleficence epistemic-injustice legal-capacity decision-making-capacity supported-decision-making substituted-decision-making psychiatric-advance-directives recovery-oriented-care trauma-informed-care safewards de-escalation coercion coercive-measures coercive-treatment restrictive-care-practices restrictive-interventions Open questions Can psychiatric coercion ever be ethically justified, or should it be abolished entirely? How do intersecting identities (e.g., gender, diagnosis) influence risk of coercive interventions? What systemic barriers prevent human rights education from changing clinical practice? How can legal and clinical frameworks better balance safety concerns with autonomy rights?

patient-safety.title: patient safety
patient-safety.plainText: patient safety Why It Matters Ensures patient well-being by preventing harm from coercive interventions Addresses systemic failures in reporting and addressing staff violence in mental health settings Highlights gaps in staff knowledge regarding legal and ethical aspects of restraint use Balances safety imperatives with respect for patient rights and autonomy Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Staff beliefs on safety | Nurses perceive physical and chemical restraint as beneficial for safety despite knowledge gaps | 2016 - Cunha et al. - Chemical and Physical Restraint of Patients | | Systemic underreporting | Violence and abuse by staff in mental health settings are significantly underreported due to systemic issues | Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 02 | | Legal and ethical knowledge deficit | Significant gaps exist in staff understanding of legal and ethical frameworks governing restraint | 2016 - Cunha et al. - Chemical and Physical Restraint of Patients | | Power imbalances and stigma | Structural factors like power differentials and societal stigma enable abuse and hinder accountability | Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 02 | Sub-topics chemical-restraint physical-restraint coercion coercion-in-psychiatry restraint restrictive-interventions seclusion de-escalation de-escalation-techniques least-restrictive-alternative Related concepts autonomy beneficence non-maleficence informed-consent human-rights person-centered-care trauma-informed-care recovery-oriented-care safewards risk-assessment Open questions How can staff training effectively bridge the gap between perceived safety benefits and actual legal/ethical knowledge of restraint use? What systemic interventions are needed to improve reporting and accountability for staff violence in mental health settings? How do power imbalances and societal stigma perpetuate unsafe practices despite existing policies and guidelines? What are the most effective strategies to reduce reliance on restraint while maintaining safety in acute psychiatric crises?

tardive-dyskinesia.title: tardive dyskinesia
tardive-dyskinesia.plainText: tardive dyskinesia Why It Matters Linked to long-term antipsychotic use and patient autonomy concerns Highlights ethical dilemmas in involuntary medication and treatment consent Represents a significant adverse effect impacting quality of life and dignity Raises legal and clinical questions about risk-benefit assessment in psychiatric care Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Constitutional rights perspective | Involuntarily hospitalized patients have a qualified right to refuse antipsychotics, overridable only if incompetent or dangerous | 1980 - Brooks - The constitutional right to refuse antipsychotic medications. | | Clinical liability perspective | Psychiatric malpractice arises from deviation from standard care; adherence to sound practice supports risk management | Clinical Manual of Psychiatry and Law - 05 | Sub-topics antipsychotics informed-consent involuntary-medication extrapyramidal-side-effects-eps Related concepts autonomy bodily-autonomy beneficence non-maleficence patient-safety risk-assessment therapeutic-alliance involuntary-treatment coercion informed-consent patient-autonomy biomedical-model dignity human-rights legal-capacity decision-making-capacity substituted-judgment advance-directives psychiatric-advance-directives least-restrictive-alternative last-resort-principle proportionality conflict-of-interest medical-malpractice clinical-decision-making evidence-based-practice recovery-model trauma-informed-care person-centered-care shared-decision-making Open questions How should the risk of tardive dyskinesia be weighed against the benefits of antipsychotic treatment in long-term care? prisma.title: PRISMA
prisma.plainText: PRISMA What It Is PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) is an evidence-based minimum set of items for reporting in systematic reviews and meta-analyses. It standardizes the reporting process to enhance transparency, reproducibility, and methodological rigor in research synthesis. Role in the Literature In 2022 - Butterworth et al. - Patients’ and staff members’ experiences of restrictive practices in acute mental health in-patient settings; systematic review and thematic synthesis: Framework followed for transparent reporting of the systematic review process, enhancing reproducibility and minimizing bias in study selection and synthesis. In 2024 - Parsaei et al. - Machine Learning for prediction of violent behaviors in schizophrenia spectrum disorders a systematic review: The reporting standard adhered to for ensuring transparency in the systematic review process.
prisma.richText:
##What It Is

PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) is an evidence-based minimum set of items for reporting in systematic reviews and meta-analyses. It standardizes the reporting process to enhance transparency, reproducibility, and methodological rigor in research synthesis.

##Role in the Literature

In {{2022 - Butterworth et al. - Patients’ and staff members’ experiences of restrictive practices in acute mental health in-patient settings; systematic review and thematic synthesis}}: Framework followed for transparent reporting of the systematic review process, enhancing reproducibility and minimizing bias in study selection and synthesis. In {{2024 - Parsaei et al. - Machine Learning for prediction of violent behaviors in schizophrenia spectrum disorders a systematic review}}: The reporting standard adhered to for ensuring transparency in the systematic review process.

medical-model.title: medical model
medical-model.plainText: medical model Why It Matters Central to understanding the historical development of antipsychotic medications and rapid tranquillisation practices Highlights tensions between biomedical reductionism and social determinants in mental health Represents a dominant paradigm critiqued for prioritizing industry interests over social justice Underpins debates about human rights, coercion, and commercialization in psychiatric care Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Biomedical reductionism | Mental illness is best understood as a brain disorder requiring pharmacological intervention | 2014 - Allison, Moncrieff - ‘Rapid tranquillisation’; an historical perspective on its emergence in the context of the development of antipsychotic medications | | Rights-based critique | Biomedical model obscures social determinants and enables industry influence, necessitating a human rights approach | 2020 - Cosgrove, shaughnessy - Mental Health as a Basic Human Right and the Interference of Commercialized Science | Sub-topics biomedical-model Related concepts antipsychotics autonomy basic-liberty beneficence coercion coercion-in-psychiatry commercialized-science human-rights human-rights-based-approach informed-consent involuntary-treatment medical-model-of-disability non-maleficence patient-autonomy person-centered-care recovery-model social-model-of-disability substantive-equality Open questions How can the biomedical model be reformed to adequately address social determinants of mental health? What mechanisms can counteract industry influence on psychiatric research and clinical guidelines? Is a pure biomedical explanation of mental illness compatible with a human rights-based approach to care? To what extent does the medical model contribute to coercive practices in psychiatric settings?

recovery-model.title: recovery model
recovery-model.plainText: recovery model Why It Matters The recovery model is a foundational concept in mental health advocacy and policy reform. It challenges coercive frameworks by emphasizing person-centered, rights-based approaches. It aligns with human rights instruments like the CRPD and ethical principles of autonomy and dignity. It informs alternatives to coercive practices in psychiatric care. recovery-model.richText:
##Why It Matters

The recovery model is a foundational concept in mental health advocacy and policy reform. It challenges coercive frameworks by emphasizing person-centered, rights-based approaches. It aligns with human rights instruments like the CRPD and ethical principles of autonomy and dignity. It informs alternatives to coercive practices in psychiatric care.

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Recovery-oriented care | Recovery is a personal journey of healing and self-determination, not merely clinical symptom reduction | {{Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 09}} | | Recovery-oriented care | Recovery requires addressing systemic injustices and power imbalances in psychiatric systems | {{The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 20}} | | Recovery-oriented care | True recovery is incompatible with coercive interventions that undermine autonomy and dignity | {{Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 09}}, {{The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 20}} |

##Sub-topics

recovery-oriented-care

##Open questions

How can recovery-oriented principles be operationalized in coercive psychiatric settings? What metrics truly reflect recovery beyond symptom reduction or compliance? How do systemic inequities (e.g., racism, poverty) intersect with access to recovery? Can recovery be meaningfully pursued under involuntary treatment orders?

advance-directives.title: advance directives
advance-directives.plainText: advance directives Why It Matters Reduces coercion by enabling prior consent and aligning care with patient preferences Honors enduring will over transient preferences in mental health contexts Supports compliance with the UN CRPD's respect for rights, will, and preferences Addresses systemic dilemmas in psychiatric care requiring legal and social reforms Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---|---|---| | Advance directives reduce coercion | Enable prior consent to align crisis interventions with patient preferences | 2012 - Bonnie - Advance Directives A Tool for Reducing Coercion | | Respect for enduring will | CRPD requires distinguishing enduring will from transient preferences; honoring prior will may override current preferences | 2017 - Szmukler - The UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities 'Rights, will and preferences' in relation to mental health disabilities | | Alternatives to coercion | Trauma-informed care, peer support, and shared decision-making reduce coercion across settings | Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 18 | | Systemic dilemmas from abolition | Abolishing psychiatric coercion creates challenges in forensic, suicide, and dementia care requiring broader reforms | Human Rights in Psychiatry - 17 | Sub-topics psychiatric-advance-directives Related concepts advance-directives, advance-directive, advance-directives-in-psychiatry, advance-directive-in-psychiatry, advance-directives-mental-health, advance-directive-mental-health, advance-directive-mental-health-care, advance-directives-mental-health-care, advance-directive-mental-health-care, advance-directives-mental-health-care Open questions How can advance directives be effectively implemented in emergency psychiatric settings? institutional-betrayal.title: institutional betrayal
institutional-betrayal.plainText: institutional betrayal Why It Matters Highlights systemic failures in institutional accountability within mental health settings Explains how harm is enabled through underreporting and inadequate responses despite known risks Connects institutional power dynamics to patient experiences of betrayal and mistrust Underscores ethical and human rights implications of coercive practices in care environments Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Systemic harm enabled by institutional failure | Abuse occurs due to underreporting, power imbalances, and stigma, not individual malice alone | Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 02 | | Coercion as structurally driven, not clinically necessary | Harmful practices stem from understaffing and cost-cutting, not patient needs | The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care - 20 | Sub-topics coercion coercion-in-psychiatry coercive-measures coercive-measures-in-psychiatry forced-medication involuntary-treatment physical-restraint seclusion trauma-informed-care Related concepts autonomy beneficence bodily-autonomy confidentiality human-rights informed-consent non-maleficence patient-autonomy patient-safety procedural-justice restorative-justice rights-based-ethics supported-decision-making therapeutic-relationship Open questions How can institutions improve accountability mechanisms for staff misconduct in psychiatric settings? What systemic reforms are needed to reduce reliance on coercive practices rooted in resource constraints? In what ways does institutional betrayal erode therapeutic alliances and long-term recovery outcomes? How do intersecting factors like stigma and structural racism amplify experiences of betrayal in care?

prospero.title: PROSPERO
prospero.plainText: PROSPERO What It Is PROSPERO (International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews) is an international database for registering systematic review protocols in health and social care. It promotes transparency, reduces duplication, and supports evidence-based practice by making protocols publicly accessible before review commencement. Role in the Literature In 2019 - Muir-Cochrane et al. - International research into 22 years of use of chemical restraint An evidence overview: hosted the protocol registration (ID: CRD 42017055258) ensuring transparency and reducing duplication. In 2020 - Bruijn et al. - Physical and Pharmacological Restraints in Hospital Care; Protocol for a Systematic Review: international prospective register of systematic reviews where this protocol was registered (CRD42019116186). In 2020 - Muir-Cochrane et al. - Prevalence of the use of chemical restraint in the management of challenging behaviours associated with adult mental health conditions A: trial registration system where the systematic review protocol (CRD42017055258) was registered. In 2022 - Butterworth et al. - Patients’ and staff members’ experiences of restrictive practices in acute mental health in-patient settings; systematic review and thematic synthesis: international prospective register of systematic reviews where the review protocol was pre-registered (CRD42020176859), ensuring transparency and reducing duplication in research.
prospero.richText:
##What It Is

PROSPERO (International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews) is an international database for registering systematic review protocols in health and social care. It promotes transparency, reduces duplication, and supports evidence-based practice by making protocols publicly accessible before review commencement.

##Role in the Literature

In {{2019 - Muir-Cochrane et al. - International research into 22 years of use of chemical restraint An evidence overview}}: hosted the protocol registration (ID: CRD 42017055258) ensuring transparency and reducing duplication. In {{2020 - Bruijn et al. - Physical and Pharmacological Restraints in Hospital Care; Protocol for a Systematic Review}}: international prospective register of systematic reviews where this protocol was registered (CRD42019116186). In {{2020 - Muir-Cochrane et al. - Prevalence of the use of chemical restraint in the management of challenging behaviours associated with adult mental health conditions A}}: trial registration system where the systematic review protocol (CRD42017055258) was registered. In {{2022 - Butterworth et al. - Patients’ and staff members’ experiences of restrictive practices in acute mental health in-patient settings; systematic review and thematic synthesis}}: international prospective register of systematic reviews where the review protocol was pre-registered (CRD42020176859), ensuring transparency and reducing duplication in research.

schizophrenia.title: schizophrenia
schizophrenia.plainText: schizophrenia Why It Matters Central focus in ethical and clinical debates about psychiatric treatment, particularly regarding decision-making capacity and involuntary care Represents a key diagnostic category where coercive interventions, such as covert medication and rapid tranquillisation, are frequently considered Highlights tensions between beneficence and autonomy in psychiatric ethics, especially in cases of noncompliance and impaired insight Serves as a focal point for examining the real-world application of ethical frameworks in managing psychosis and behavioral disturbances Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|-----------|-------------| | Ethical justification of covert antipsychotic use | Covert administration may reduce harmful behaviors (e.g., foreign object ingestion) in patients with partial decision-making capacity, despite failing to control core psychosis and risking side effects | 2024 - Razzak et al. - Ethical Dilemmas in Covert Prescribing A Case Report on Medication Administration for Noncompliant Psychiatric Patients | | Historical context of rapid tranquillisation | Rapid tranquillisation emerged alongside antipsychotic development as a response to acute agitation, reflecting evolving practices in managing psychiatric emergencies | 2014 - Allison, Moncrieff - ‘Rapid tranquillisation’; an historical perspective on its emergence in the context of the development of antipsychotic medications | Sub-topics antipsychotics decision-making-capacity rapid-ttranquillisation extrapyramidal-side-effects-eps coercion-in-psychiatry informed-consent beneficence autonomy Related concepts advance-directives civil-commitment clinical-ethics coercive-measures community-treatment-order confidentiality forced-medication involuntary-treatment least-restrictive-principle patient-safety psychiatric-advance-directives psychiatric-emergency psychiatric-reform recovery-model substituted-decision-making treatment-gap Open questions Under what conditions, if any, is covert medication ethically permissible in schizophrenia when it addresses behavioral risks but not psychotic symptoms? How should clinicians balance historical practices of rapid tranquillisation with contemporary standards of consent and least restrictive alternatives? What safeguards are needed to prevent misuse of pharmacological interventions in schizophrenia while preserving therapeutic intent? How can decision-making capacity be assessed fairly in schizophrenia when insight into illness is often impaired by the condition itself?
schizophrenia.richText:
##Why It Matters

Central focus in ethical and clinical debates about psychiatric treatment, particularly regarding decision-making capacity and involuntary care Represents a key diagnostic category where coercive interventions, such as covert medication and rapid tranquillisation, are frequently considered Highlights tensions between beneficence and autonomy in psychiatric ethics, especially in cases of noncompliance and impaired insight Serves as a focal point for examining the real-world application of ethical frameworks in managing psychosis and behavioral disturbances

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|-----------|-------------| | Ethical justification of covert antipsychotic use | Covert administration may reduce harmful behaviors (e.g., foreign object ingestion) in patients with partial decision-making capacity, despite failing to control core psychosis and risking side effects | {{2024 - Razzak et al. - Ethical Dilemmas in Covert Prescribing A Case Report on Medication Administration for Noncompliant Psychiatric Patients}} | | Historical context of rapid tranquillisation | Rapid tranquillisation emerged alongside antipsychotic development as a response to acute agitation, reflecting evolving practices in managing psychiatric emergencies | {{2014 - Allison, Moncrieff - ‘Rapid tranquillisation’; an historical perspective on its emergence in the context of the development of antipsychotic medications}} |

##Sub-topics

antipsychotics decision-making-capacity rapid-ttranquillisation extrapyramidal-side-effects-eps coercion-in-psychiatry informed-consent beneficence autonomy

##Open questions

Under what conditions, if any, is covert medication ethically permissible in schizophrenia when it addresses behavioral risks but not psychotic symptoms? How should clinicians balance historical practices of rapid tranquillisation with contemporary standards of consent and least restrictive alternatives? What safeguards are needed to prevent misuse of pharmacological interventions in schizophrenia while preserving therapeutic intent? How can decision-making capacity be assessed fairly in schizophrenia when insight into illness is often impaired by the condition itself?

substantive-equality.title: substantive equality
substantive-equality.plainText: substantive equality Why It Matters Central to interpreting CRPD Article 12 on legal capacity as requiring equality in practice, not just formal rights Bridges civil-political and socio-economic rights through the indivisibility principle Supports dignity by recognizing that autonomy requires both internal capacity and external support Positions supported decision-making as normative and substitute decision-making as a last resort Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Supported decision-making as norm | Legal capacity "on an equal basis with others" requires inclusive equality, making supported decision-making the standard approach to uphold dignity and substantive equality | Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -06 | | Substitute decision-making as last resort | Substitute decision-making should only be used for those with severe cognitive impairment when supported decision-making is insufficient, guided by dignity and indivisibility | Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -07 | | Autonomy as achievement with support | Autonomy under CRPD Article 12 is not innate but achieved through a combination of internal competencies and external support, integrating rights via indivisibility | Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -04 | | Indivisibility of rights | Civil-political rights (like legal capacity) and socio-economic rights must be treated as interconnected to realize substantive equality under the CRPD | Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -04 | Sub-topics legal-capacity supported-decision-making substitute-decision-making principle-of-indivisibility human-dignity crpd-article-12 autonomy relational-autonomy Related concepts decision-making-capacity informed-consent legal-capacity supported-decision-making substituted-judgment human-rights disability-rights ethical-decision-making Open questions How can systems effectively determine when supported decision-making is insufficient and substitute decision-making becomes necessary without undermining the individual’s dignity? What practical mechanisms ensure that support for decision-making is truly accessible and not merely procedural, especially in resource-limited settings? To what extent does the interpretation of "equal basis with others" in CRPD Article 12 require addressing structural socio-economic inequalities beyond individual support measures? How should legal systems balance the indivisibility of rights when socio-economic supports needed for substantive equality are not adequately provided by the state?

##Key Positions

| Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Supported decision-making as norm | Legal capacity "on an equal basis with others" requires inclusive equality, making supported decision-making the standard approach to uphold dignity and substantive equality | {{Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -06}} | | Substitute decision-making as last resort | Substitute decision-making should only be used for those with severe cognitive impairment when supported decision-making is insufficient, guided by dignity and indivisibility | {{Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -07}} | | Autonomy as achievement with support | Autonomy under CRPD Article 12 is not innate but achieved through a combination of internal competencies and external support, integrating rights via indivisibility | {{Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -04}} | | Indivisibility of rights | Civil-political rights (like legal capacity) and socio-economic rights must be treated as interconnected to realize substantive equality under the CRPD | {{Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -04}} |
