world-health-organization-who.title: World Health Organization (WHO)
world-health-organization-who.plainText: World Health Organization (WHO) What It Is The World Health Organization (WHO) is a specialized agency of the United Nations responsible for international public health. It sets global health standards, provides technical guidance, and leads initiatives on mental health, human rights, and healthcare quality, making it a central authority in shaping policies and practices worldwide. Role in the Literature In 2005 - Hunt - Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Right of Everyone to the Enjoyment of the Highest Attainable Standard of Physical and Mental Health, Paul Hunt: cited as a key source of reports, guidelines, and initiatives such as the Mental Health Atlas 2014, MiNDbank, QualityRights initiative, and the World Health Report 2001. In 2014 - Mfoafo-M'Carthy et al. - Human Rights Violations and Mental Illness: cited for global mental health burden estimates, human rights project, and policy frameworks. In 2016 - Cunha et al. - Chemical and Physical Restraint of Patients: cited for its definition of aggressiveness used in the study's introduction to contextualize behavioral risks. In 2020 - Bruijn et al. - Physical and Pharmacological Restraints in Hospital Care; Protocol for a Systematic Review: referenced in relation to the International Clinical Trials Registry Platform (ICTRP), which will be used to assess outcome reporting bias. In 2020 - Cosgrove, shaughnessy - Mental Health as a Basic Human Right and the Interference of Commercialized Science: cited for its definition of health as encompassing mental well-being and its recognition of mental health as integral to the right to health. In 2020 - Rodrigues - Implementing Alternatives to Coercion; A Key Component of Improving Mental Health Care: developed the Quality Rights Initiative, a key framework cited for reducing coercion through human rights-based training and assessment. In 2024 - Galderisi et al. - Ethical challenges in contemporary psychiatry an overview and an appraisal of possible strategies and research needs: defined telepsychiatry and referenced in discussions of digital psychiatry's global implications and opportunities. In 2024 - Laureano et al. - Ethical Issues in Clinical Decision-Making about Involuntary Psychiatric Treatment A Scoping Review: referenced for its Involuntary Admission and Treatment checklist used to compare international detention criteria. In 2024 - Savage et al. - Comparison of coercive practices in worldwide mental healthcare; overcoming difficulties resulting from variations in monitoring strategies: provider of the Mental Health Atlas dataset, which currently only tracks involuntary hospital admissions as a proxy for coercion; the study recommends expanding it to include comprehensive restrictive. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 04: cited for promoting recovery-oriented and cooperation-based mental health services. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 05: central to global mental health policy, driving the Quality Rights Initiative that obliges member states to center human rights in service provision and provides the foundational definition of health. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 09: UN agency issuing guidelines that call for avoidance of coercion and respect for human rights in psychiatry. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 10: developer of the QualityRights Initiative to align mental health law and practice with CRPD standards. In Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 03: cited for policy influence through resolutions promoting people-centred care and health system responsiveness.

international-covenant-on-economic-socia.title: International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (ICESCR)
international-covenant-on-economic-socia.plainText: International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (ICESCR) What It Is The International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (ICESCR) is a United Nations treaty that obligates states to progressively realize economic, social, and cultural rights, including health and an adequate standard of living. It is a foundational instrument in international human rights law, establishing legally binding commitments that underpin state obligations in areas such as mental health and social welfare. Role in the Literature In 2005 - Hunt - Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Right of Everyone to the Enjoyment of the Highest Attainable Standard of Physical and Mental Health, Paul Hunt: Cited as the legally binding basis for the right to mental health, underpinning state obligations to respect, protect, and fulfill this right through progressive realization. In 2019 - Raveesh et al. - How right is right-based mental health law: Cited as one of the core international human rights treaties that, along with others, informs the context of rights protections relevant to mental health. In Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -03: Described as the counterpart to ICCPR, focusing on socio-economic rights. In Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -05: Cited similarly to ICCPR for its recognition of inherent dignity in human rights frameworks.

ajob-neuroscience.title: AJOB Neuroscience
ajob-neuroscience.plainText: AJOB Neuroscience What It Is AJOB Neuroscience is a peer-reviewed journal focused on the ethical, legal, and social implications of neuroscience research and clinical applications. It serves as a key venue for interdisciplinary discourse at the intersection of neuroscience, bioethics, and clinical practice. Its publications contribute significantly to shaping debates on emerging neurotechnologies and their societal impacts. Role in the Literature In 2024 - Birks - Chemical Restraints and the Basic Liberties: Publication venue for the article discussing ethical implications of chemical restraints. In 2024 - Crutchfield, Redinger - Basic Liberties, Consent, and Chemical Restraints: Peer-reviewed journal where the article was published, establishing its relevance to neuroethics and clinical neuroscience discourse. In 2024 - Dougherty et al. - The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint; Prioritizing Patient Experience: Peer-reviewed journal publishing the commentary that critiques and engages with the arguments presented by Crutchfield and Redinger.

pubmed.title: PubMed
pubmed.plainText: PubMed What It Is PubMed is a free, comprehensive biomedical literature database maintained by the National Institutes of Health (NIH). It provides access to millions of citations and abstracts from peer-reviewed journals in medicine, nursing, dentistry, veterinary medicine, and the life sciences. In the cited literature, it serves as a critical source for identifying relevant studies through systematic literature searches. Role in the Literature In 2014 - Mfoafo-M'Carthy et al. - Human Rights Violations and Mental Illness: used as the database for conducting the literature search on human rights violations and mental illness In 2024 - Parsaei et al. - Machine Learning for prediction of violent behaviors in schizophrenia spectrum disorders a systematic review: one of the primary literature sources searched to identify relevant studies on machine learning applications in schizophrenia spectrum disorders

committee-on-the-rights-of-persons-with-.title: Committee on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities
committee-on-the-rights-of-persons-with-.plainText: Committee on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities What It Is The Committee on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities is a UN expert body responsible for monitoring state implementation of the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD). It interprets the CRPD’s provisions, issues general comments, and reviews country reports to promote compliance with disability rights standards, particularly in areas such as legal capacity and mental health treatment. Role in the Literature In 2005 - Hunt - Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Right of Everyone to the Enjoyment of the Highest Attainable Standard of Physical and Mental Health, Paul Hunt: Interpreted the CRPD, especially regarding legal capacity and the absolute prohibition of involuntary treatment, offering authoritative guidance on state compliance. In 2019 - Scholten et al. - Psychiatric Advance Directives Under the Convention on the Rights of Persons With Disabilities Why Advance Instructions Should Be Able to: The UN body whose authoritative interpretation of the CRPD is analyzed as promoting the radical model that undermines PADs. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 09: UN body that reviews state implementation of the CRPD and has issued critiques of Germany and Switzerland.

convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with.title: Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD)
convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-with.plainText: Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) What It Is The Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) is an international human rights treaty adopted by the United Nations in 2006, establishing a comprehensive framework for the protection and promotion of the rights of persons with disabilities. It is central to contemporary discourse on mental health and human rights, particularly for its emphasis on legal capacity, autonomy, and the rejection of coercive practices in psychiatry. Role in the Literature In 2005 - Hunt - Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Right of Everyone to the Enjoyment of the Highest Attainable Standard of Physical and Mental Health, Paul Hunt: provides the legal foundation for redefining mental health rights, focusing on legal capacity, freedom from coercion, and community inclusion. In 2019 - Puras et al. - Mental health and human rights in the 21st century: establishes the human rights model of disability and obligates states to end forced treatment and substitute decision-making in mental health. In 2021 - Every‐Palmer et al. - Applying Human Rights and Reducing Coercion in Psychiatry following Service User-Led Education; A Qualitative Study.: serves as the theoretical foundation for training, especially through Article 12 on legal capacity and supported decision-making. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 05: cited as the most recent and critical international treaty relevant to coercion reduction, particularly Article 12 on legal capacity, which challenges substitute decision-making and promotes supported decision-making. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 12: referenced for its principles opposing involuntary admission by emphasizing autonomy, dignity, and freedom of choice for persons with disabilities. In Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -03: identified as the primary treaty under analysis, particularly regarding Article 12 on legal capacity. In Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -05: analyzed as the central international human rights treaty for its treatment of dignity, especially in Articles 1, 3, and 12.

international-covenant-on-civil-and-poli.title: International Covenant on Civil and Prisoner Rights (ICCPR)
international-covenant-on-civil-and-poli.plainText: International Covenant on Civil and Prisoner Rights (ICCPR) What It Is The International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR) is a multilateral treaty adopted by the United Nations in 1966, forming one of the two core international human rights covenants alongside the International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights. It establishes legally binding obligations for states to respect and ensure civil and political rights, including the right to life, freedom of expression, and liberty and security of person. In the cited literature, it serves as the foundational legal framework for interpreting human rights protections in mental health and capacity-related contexts, particularly through its provisions on dignity, non-discrimination, and due process. Role in the Literature In 2019 - Martin, Gurbai - Surveying the Geneva impasse; Coercive care and human rights: The treaty provides the legal basis for the Human Rights Committee’s proportionality analysis under Article 9 regarding liberty and security of person in coercive care settings. In 2019 - Raveesh et al. - How right is right-based mental health law: It is cited as part of the international human rights framework supporting legal claims for rights-based approaches in mental health legislation. In Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -03: Identified as one of the two treaties that transformed the Universal Declaration of Human Rights into binding international law. In Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -05: Referenced for its incorporation of the UDHR’s principle of inherent dignity into enforceable human rights norms.

It establishes legally binding obligations for states to respect and ensure civil and political rights, including the right to life, freedom of expression, and liberty and security of person. In the cited literature, it serves as the foundational legal framework for interpreting human rights protections in mental health and capacity-related contexts, particularly through its provisions on dignity, non-discrimination, and due process.

##Role in the Literature

In {{2019 - Martin, Gurbai - Surveying the Geneva impasse; Coercive care and human rights}}: The treaty provides the legal basis for the Human Rights Committee’s proportionality analysis under Article 9 regarding liberty and security of person in coercive care settings. In {{Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -03}}: Identified as one of the two treaties that transformed the Universal Declaration of Human Rights into binding international law. european-court-of-human-rights.title: European Court of Human Rights
european-court-of-human-rights.plainText: European Court of Human Rights What It Is The European Court of Human Rights (ECtHR) is an international court that interprets and applies the European Convention on Human Rights, ensuring compliance by member states. It plays a central role in safeguarding fundamental rights, including protections against inhuman or degrading treatment under Article 3, and its jurisprudence shapes legal standards across Europe, particularly in contexts involving mental health and state obligations. Role in the Literature In 2022 - O'Donovan et al. - Current trends in restrictive interventions in psychiatry; a European perspective: The ECtHR's jurisprudence on Article 3 is cited as a key legal framework shaping interpretations of when psychiatric restraint constitutes prohibited treatment. In Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -03: The ECtHR is referenced as an example of indirect enforcement of socio-economic rights through civil-political mechanisms, illustrated in the case Airey v Ireland.

embase.title: Embase
embase.plainText: Embase What It Is Embase is a biomedical and pharmacological database that indexes peer-reviewed literature, including journal articles, conference proceedings, and drug information. It is widely used in systematic reviews due to its comprehensive coverage of clinical and biomedical research, particularly in Europe and globally. Role in the Literature In 2024 - Laureano et al. - Ethical Issues in Clinical Decision-Making about Involuntary Psychiatric Treatment A Scoping Review: One of five databases searched for relevant literature In 2024 - Parsaei et al. - Machine Learning for prediction of violent behaviors in schizophrenia spectrum disorders a systematic review: A biomedical and pharmacological database used in the literature search

national-health-service-nhs.title: National Health Service (NHS)
national-health-service-nhs.plainText: National Health Service (NHS) What It Is The National Health Service (NHS) is the publicly funded healthcare system of the United Kingdom, providing universal medical services to residents. It operates as a central state institution responsible for delivering health and social care, with its structure and accountability shaped by legislation such as the Human Rights Act 1998. Role in the Literature In 2008 - Lepping - Is psychiatry torn in different ethical directions: Referenced as the UK public health system whose relationship with citizens is mediated by the Human Rights Act 1998, illustrating legal and ethical frameworks governing psychiatric practice. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 02: Described as the healthcare system where documented incidents of coercion and violence occurred, providing the institutional context for mental health service delivery in the UK.

It operates as a central state institution responsible for delivering health and social care, with its structure and accountability shaped by legislation such as the Human Rights Act 1998.

##Role in the Literature

In {{2008 - Lepping - Is psychiatry torn in different ethical directions}}: Referenced as the UK public health system whose relationship with citizens is mediated by the Human Rights Act 1998, illustrating legal and ethical frameworks governing psychiatric practice. In {{Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 02}}: Described as the healthcare system where documented incidents of coercion and violence occurred, providing the institutional context for mental health service delivery in the UK.

world-bank.title: World Bank
world-bank.plainText: World Bank What It Is The World Bank is an international financial institution that provides loans and grants to governments of low- and middle-income countries for development projects. It operates in the domain of global economic development, poverty reduction, and institutional reform, influencing policy and funding in sectors such as health, education, and infrastructure. Role in the Literature In 2005 - Hunt - Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Right of Everyone to the Enjoyment of the Highest Attainable Standard of Physical and Mental Health, Paul Hunt: referenced for its involvement in mental health financing and development cooperation, with caution regarding its potential prioritization of institutional reform over community-based, rights-based approaches. In 2014 - Mfoafo-M'Carthy et al. - Human Rights Violations and Mental Illness: cited for using its income classification thresholds in the analysis of mental illness and human rights violations across countries.

international-classification-of-diseases.title: International Classification of Diseases (ICD)
international-classification-of-diseases.plainText: International Classification of Diseases (ICD) What It Is The International Classification of Diseases (ICD) is a globally standardized diagnostic tool developed by the World Health Organization for classifying diseases, disorders, and health conditions. It serves as the foundational framework for psychiatric diagnosis and medical coding, underpinning clinical practice, epidemiology, and health policy worldwide. Role in the Literature In 2023 - Oute et al. - Conflict and antagonism within global psychiatry A discourse analysis of organisational responses to the UN reports on rights-based approaches in: Referenced by multiple organizations to assert psychiatry's legitimacy as a medical discipline In Human Rights in Psychiatry - 17: Implied basis for ICD-linked ICF assessments; represents the diagnostic infrastructure that would need reform to support diagnosis-independent support services.

mysore-declaration.title: Mysore Declaration
mysore-declaration.plainText: Mysore Declaration What It Is The Mysore Declaration is a regional declaration from the 2013 international symposium in Mysore, India, focused on mental health rights and the elimination of coercive practices in mental health care. It emerged as a foundational reference for rights-based approaches in Indian mental health policy, influencing later legislation such as the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017. The declaration emphasizes dignity, autonomy, and the reduction of discrimination and coercion in mental health settings. Role in the Literature In 2019 - Raveesh et al. - How right is right-based mental health law: Referenced as a regional declaration that reinforces the rights-based ethos underpinning the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, particularly in emphasizing the elimination of discrimination and coercion. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 03: Described as the outcome of the 2013 international symposium in Mysore, India, where it defined coercive measures in the Indian context and outlined strategies to minimize coercion in mental health settings.

cinahl.title: CINAHL
cinahl.plainText: CINAHL What It Is CINAHL is a comprehensive database of nursing and allied health literature, indexing journals, books, and evidence-based resources in healthcare. It is widely used for systematic and integrative reviews in clinical practice, particularly in mental health and nursing research. Role in the Literature In 2018 - Cusack et al. - An integrative review exploring the physical and psychological harm inherent in using restraint in mental health inpatient settings: One of five databases searched for relevant literature. In 2019 - Muir-Cochrane et al. - International research into 22 years of use of chemical restraint An evidence overview: Database searched for nursing and allied health literature relevant to restraint practices in clinical settings. In 2020 - Muir-Cochrane et al. - Prevalence of the use of chemical restraint in the management of challenging behaviours associated with adult mental health conditions A: Nursing and allied health literature database searched for relevant studies. In 2024 - Bennetts et al. - Elimination of restrictive practices from acute adult mental health care services; A qualitative evidence synthesis of the lived experience literature.: One of the primary databases searched for nursing and allied health literature on restrictive practices in mental health settings. In 2024 - Laureano et al. - Ethical Issues in Clinical Decision-Making about Involuntary Psychiatric Treatment A Scoping Review: One of five databases searched for relevant literature.

frontiers-in-psychiatry.title: Frontiers in Psychiatry
frontiers-in-psychiatry.plainText: Frontiers in Psychiatry What It Is Frontiers in Psychiatry is a peer-reviewed, open-access scientific journal that publishes original research, reviews, and clinical studies in the field of psychiatry and mental health. It serves as a key venue for disseminating evidence-based findings relevant to psychiatric disorders, treatment approaches, and neuroscience, contributing to advancements in both academic and clinical practice. Role in the Literature In 2021 - Searles Quick et al. - Which Emergent Medication Should I Give Next Repeated Use of Emergent Medications to Treat Acute Agitation: the peer-reviewed journal that published this manuscript In 2024 - Parsaei et al. - Machine Learning for prediction of violent behaviors in schizophrenia spectrum disorders a systematic review: the peer-reviewed journal where the study was published

un-convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-w.title: UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD)
un-convention-on-the-rights-of-persons-w.plainText: UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) What It Is The UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) is an international human rights treaty adopted in 2006 to promote, protect, and ensure the full and equal enjoyment of all human rights by persons with disabilities. It establishes legal standards for inclusion, accessibility, and non-discrimination, and is central to debates on eliminating coercive practices in mental health care. Role in the Literature In 2018 - Cusack et al. - An integrative review exploring the physical and psychological harm inherent in using restraint in mental health inpatient settings: Cited regarding the potential unlawfulness of compulsion and coercion under the CRPD’s human rights framework. In 2019 - Martin, Gurbai - Surveying the Geneva impasse; Coercive care and human rights: Interpreted by the CRPD Committee as establishing an absolute right against liberty deprivation based on disability under Article 14. In Human Rights in Psychiatry - 17: Described as the central international treaty driving the abolition of coercion in psychiatry and framing the core legal obligation examined.

american-psychiatric-association.title: American Psychiatric Association
american-psychiatric-association.plainText: American Psychiatric Association What It Is The American Psychiatric Association (APA) is a professional organization of psychiatrists in the United States, responsible for developing and publishing the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM). It is a central authority in psychiatric practice and research, shaping diagnostic criteria and clinical standards widely used in mental health care. Role in the Literature In 2012 - Bonnie - Advance Directives A Tool for Reducing Coercion: Publisher of Psychiatric Services, providing the professional and institutional context for the article’s dissemination to mental health practitioners. In 2020 - Cosgrove, shaughnessy - Mental Health as a Basic Human Right and the Interference of Commercialized Science: Identified as the developer of the DSM and criticized for its members’ extensive financial ties to pharmaceutical companies, raising concerns about undue industry influence on psychiatric taxonomy. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 09: Professional organization involved in exchanges with UN rapporteurs regarding torture and coercion in psychiatric settings.

joint-commission-on-accreditation-of-hea.title: Joint Commission on Accreditation of Healthcare Organization (JCAHO)
joint-commission-on-accreditation-of-hea.plainText: Joint Commission on Accreditation of Healthcare Organization (JCAHO) What It Is The Joint Commission on Accreditation of Healthcare Organization (JCAHO) is a U.S.-based nonprofit organization that accredits and certifies healthcare organizations and programs. It sets standards for quality and safety in healthcare, influencing clinical practices across hospitals and psychiatric facilities through its accreditation requirements. Role in the Literature In 2003 - Currier - The Controversy over “Chemical Restraint” in Acute Care Psychiatry: JCAHO's accreditation standards are cited as influencing hospital practices regarding the use of chemical restraints in acute psychiatry. In 2017 - Negroni - On the concept of restraint in psychiatry: JCAHO is referenced for its role in shaping definitional and regulatory frameworks around restraint use in psychiatric care.

perceived-coercion-scale-pcs.title: Perceived Coercion Scale (PCS)
perceived-coercion-scale-pcs.plainText: Perceived Coercion Scale (PCS) What It Is The Perceived Coercion Scale (PCS) is a 5-item self-report tool designed to measure subjective feelings of coercion in psychiatric care, adapted from the MacArthur Admission Experience Survey for Korean cultural contexts. It assesses patients’ perceptions of whether their hospitalization aligned with their own will, using a 4-point Likert scale, and is widely used to evaluate the subjective experience of coercion in mental health treatment. Role in the Literature In 2012 - Seo et al. - Coercion in psychiatric care Can paternalism justify coercion: Used to measure subjective feelings of coercion via five items on a 4-point Likert scale, adapted from the MacArthur Admission Experience Survey for Korean culture. In Coercive Treatment in Psychiatry - 19: Employed as a central measure to assess outcomes by evaluating the subjective experience of coercion in psychiatric treatment settings.

united-nations-convention-on-the-rights-.title: United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD)
united-nations-convention-on-the-rights-.plainText: United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) What It Is The United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) is an international human rights treaty that promotes, protects, and ensures the full and equal enjoyment of all human rights by persons with disabilities. It is central to discussions in the literature on mental health law, particularly concerning legal capacity, involuntary treatment, and supported decision-making. Role in the Literature In [[2019 - Scholten et al. - Psychiatric Advance Directives Under the Convention on the Rights of Persons With Disabilities Why Advance Instructions Should Be Able to]: The international human rights treaty whose interpretation is central to the analysis of PAD effectiveness In [[2024 - Birkeland et al. - Abolition of coercion in mental health services - A European survey of feasibility]: International human rights framework emphasizing autonomy and liberty; cited as a foundation for abolition efforts in mental health In [[2024 - Galderisi et al. - Ethical challenges in contemporary psychiatry an overview and an appraisal of possible strategies and research needs]: Central framework analyzed for implications on psychiatric practice, particularly regarding legal capacity, involuntary treatment, and supported decision-making In [[2024 - Laureano et al. - Ethical Issues in Clinical Decision-Making about Involuntary Psychiatric Treatment A Scoping Review]: Central legal and ethical framework referenced throughout the analysis of involuntary treatment practices In [[Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 10]: Central framework used to critique involuntary admission as discriminatory and a violation of legal equality In [[Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -01]: The core international human rights treaty analyzed throughout the text, particularly Article 12 on legal capacity In [[Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -06]: The international human rights treaty establishing rights for persons with disabilities, including Article 12 on legal capacity In [[Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -07]: The central international human rights treaty governing disability rights, especially Article 12 on legal capacity

american-medical-association-ama.title: American Medical Association (AMA)
american-medical-association-ama.plainText: American Medical Association (AMA) What It Is The American Medical Association (AMA) is a professional organization representing physicians in the United States, established to promote medical science, public health, and the art of medicine. It is widely recognized for developing and maintaining the AMA Code of Medical Ethics, which serves as a foundational guide for ethical conduct in clinical practice. The AMA's ethical frameworks are frequently referenced in biomedical literature as authoritative sources for understanding physician responsibilities and patient rights. Role in the Literature In 2016 - Sidhu, Srinivasraghavan - Ethics and medical practice; Why psychiatry is unique: The AMA's historical and current medical ethics code is cited as a derivative of early medical ethics frameworks that inform foundational principles in psychiatric practice. In 2022 - Mueller - Ethical and Legal Concerns Associated With Withdrawing Mechanical Circulatory Support; A U.S. Perspective: The AMA Code of Ethics is referenced for its guidance on withdrawing non-beneficial treatment, particularly in the context of mechanical circulatory support decisions.
